Once a year, on the first Sunday of April, that quiet devotion explodes into the Kanamara Matsuri, when three phallus mikoshi — a wooden one, an iron one donated by a shipbuilder, and the unmistakable hot-pink "Elizabeth" carried by a cross-dressing club — are paraded through the streets beneath the cherry blossoms. It draws crowds from around the world, and in recent decades the festival has folded in messages of sexual health and gender diversity. Strange, yes — but it is a working shrine, and the laughter is meant to sit alongside genuine prayer, not replace it.
highlights_en: A metal phallus as the literal object of worship — one of Japan's most direct surviving forms of phallic religion
Three phallus mikoshi paraded at the Kanamara Matsuri: a wooden one, an iron one, and the famous pink 'Elizabeth'
Rooted in the myth of these gods nursing Izanami's burns, fusing phallic worship with earnest prayers for fertility, safe birth, and recovery from disease

name: 鹿野大仏（寳光寺）
name_kana: ろくやだいぶつ（ほうこうじ）
address: 〒190-0182 東京都西多摩郡日の出町平井3392地先
fee: 大人（中学生以上）300円、小人（小学生）100円
summary: 東京都日の出町の曹洞宗・寳光寺が、裏山の鹿野山中腹に2018年に完成させた銅造釈迦如来坐像。像高約12メートル、台座を含めると約18メートルで、坐仏としては国内有数の大きさとされる。鎌倉大仏（約11.3メートル）を上回る像高を持つ「平成の大仏」として、完成直後から注目を集めた。歴代住職の発願を世代を超えて受け継ぎ、山形の鋳造業者と翠雲堂が手がけた新しい巨大仏で、伝統的な大仏造立が現代にも継続していることを示す事例である。亀山市からは中央道経由で日帰り遠征も可能な圏内。
highlights: 2018年完成の「平成の大仏」、像高12メートル・総高18メートルの新造巨大仏
鎌倉大仏を上回る像高を持つ金色の銅造釈迦如来坐像
山中腹に忽然と現れる巨像と、麓の寺・参道からの登りのコントラスト
from_kameyama: 車で約5〜6時間（新名神・東名・圏央道経由）。日帰り強行は可能だが余裕を持つなら一泊推奨。
summary_en: Most of Japan's great Buddhas are centuries old — relics of medieval faith or ancient state power. This one was finished in 2018. On the slope of Mount Rokuya behind Hōkō-ji, a Sōtō Zen temple in the mountain town of Hinode just west of central Tokyo, sits a gilded bronze Shakyamuni Buddha about 12 meters tall, roughly 18 meters with its pedestal — tall enough to edge out the famous Great Buddha of Kamakura. It is, in effect, a 21st-century great Buddha, and the strangeness lies in encountering something so monumental and so new at the same time, materializing on a quiet wooded hillside reached by climbing a kilometer of mountain path from the temple below. The project was a vow held across generations: the 32nd abbot pledged to build it, died in 1995, and his successors finally realized it after the 2011 earthquake, using a Yamagata foundry and traditional cast-bronze technique. It is proof that Japan's tradition of building colossal Buddhas never really ended — it just went quiet for a while.
highlights_en: A "Heisei-era great Buddha" completed in 2018 — 12 meters for the figure, 18 meters with the pedestal
A gilded cast-bronze seated Shakyamuni whose figure height exceeds the Great Buddha of Kamakura
A colossus appearing suddenly on a mountainside, reached by climbing up from the temple and approach path below

name: 越後胎内観音
name_kana: えちごたいないかんのん
fee: 観音像参拝自由・帰林殿（童女石拝観）有料
summary: 新潟県胎内市、鳥坂山を背に立つ総丈7.3メートル・重量4トンの青銅製観音像。1967年（昭和42年）8月28日の羽越大水害（羽越豪雨）の殉難者を弔い、災害復興と平和を祈念して1970年（昭和45年）8月28日に建立された。八葉の蓮華台に合掌して立つ姿が波の上に乗っているように見えることから「波乗り観音」とも呼ばれる。境内の帰林殿には、水害で犠牲になった子どもの姿が浮かぶと伝えられる「童女石」が安置され、災害の記憶を継承する民俗的な場として機能している。樽ヶ橋・道の駅胎内に近く、水害の慰霊と地域の祈りが重なる場所である。
highlights: 羽越大水害の殉難者慰霊として建立された総丈7.3メートルの青銅観音
蓮華台が波に乗るように見える「波乗り観音」の造形
水害で亡くなった子どもの顔が浮かぶと伝わる「童女石」を祀る帰林殿
from_kameyama: 鉄道で約6〜7時間（亀山→名古屋→上越新幹線で新潟、羽越本線で中条駅、駅から車・タクシーで約10分）。車なら高速利用で約7時間と遠隔地。
summary_en: Against the wooded flank of Mount Torisaka in Tainai City, a 7.3-meter bronze Kannon stands with palms pressed together atop a lotus throne — and the throne seems to ride on cresting waves, which earned the figure its local name, the 'Wave-Riding Kannon.' Its serenity is rooted in catastrophe. On 28 August 1967, the Uetsu Flood Disaster tore through the Kaetsu region of Niigata and neighboring Yamagata, killing and leaving missing well over a hundred people. Three years later, to the day, the statue was consecrated to console the dead, to pray for recovery, and to ask for future peace — its anniversary deliberately set on the day of the flood. Nearby, in the Kirin-den hall, sits the site's quietest and strangest object: the 'Maiden Stone,' a piece of rock in whose surface mourners say they can make out the faces of children lost to the water. It is less a ghost story than a community's grief pressed into stone — a place where the memory of a disaster, folk belief, and an act of public prayer all meet at the foot of a single bronze figure.
highlights_en: A 7.3-meter bronze Kannon erected as a memorial to the victims of the 1967 Uetsu Flood Disaster
The 'Wave-Riding Kannon' design, its lotus pedestal seeming to ride atop the waves
The Kirin-den hall enshrining the 'Maiden Stone,' said to show the faces of children lost in the flood

name: 白馬大仏
name_kana: はくばだいぶつ
address: 新潟県糸魚川市大字大所989-6
status: 現存（旧白馬観光ホテルは閉館・解体、大仏像は現存）
fee: 無料（拝観路は山中・通行状況は要確認）
hours: 屋外・常時（参道・周辺施設の管理状況により制限あり）
summary: 新潟・長野県境に近い糸魚川市大所、姫川沿いの山腹に鎮座する高さ約23.5メートル（台座共）の白い坐像。宗教法人白馬大仏教会の管長で白馬観光ホテル社長でもあった金田義孝が建立し、1969年に開眼、1972年に入魂式が行われた。眼球と眉間の白毫に翡翠、唇に瑪瑙を用いた特異な造作で知られる。母体となった白馬観光ホテルや周辺の温泉施設は既に閉鎖・解体され、1995年の水害後に国道が大仏前から離れて付け替えられたこともあって訪れる人は大きく減った。それでも純白の大仏は今も山中に静かに座り続けている。
highlights: 深い山あいの斜面に突如現れる高さ約23.5mの純白の坐像という強い違和感
眼と白毫に翡翠、唇に瑪瑙を用いた手の込んだ素材使い
母体だった観光ホテル・温泉が消えても残り続ける『遺された大仏』という佇まい
from_kameyama: 車で東名阪・新名神・北陸道経由、糸魚川IC方面から約4時間30分（約340km）。鉄道はJR大糸線「平岩駅」が最寄りだが本数が少なく、車利用が現実的。
summary_en: Drive the winding valley road along the Himekawa River toward the Niigata–Nagano border and a pure-white Buddha suddenly materializes on the green mountainside above you, 23.5 meters tall and utterly out of place. The Hakuba Daibutsu was raised in the late 1960s by Kaneda Yoshitaka — a man who was simultaneously the head priest of his own religious corporation and the president of the Hakuba Kanko Hotel — and consecrated in 1972. Its details are extravagant: the eyes and the white mark between the brows are carved from jade (a fitting touch in Itoigawa, Japan's famed jade country) and the lips are agate. It was built to draw guests to the hotel and its hot springs, faith and tourism fused into a single colossal figure. But the hotel has since closed and been demolished, a 1995 flood pushed the national highway away from the statue, and the surrounding spa buildings shut down one by one. The visitors drained away. What remains is the strangest and quietest thing of all: a giant white Buddha, abandoned by the resort that built it, still sitting alone on the mountain and watching over an empty valley.
highlights_en: A 23.5-meter pure-white seated Buddha that appears with startling incongruity on a remote mountainside
Lavish materials — jade eyes and forehead mark, agate lips — echoing Itoigawa's identity as Japan's jade country
The melancholy of an 'abandoned tourism Buddha' that outlived the hotel and hot springs built around it

name: 万治の石仏
name_kana: まんじのせきぶつ
address: 〒393-0031 長野県諏訪郡下諏訪町東山田字石仏
fee: 無料（拝観自由）
summary: 諏訪大社下社春宮の近く、砥川を渡った先に鎮座する江戸前期の石仏。巨大な安山岩の自然石をそのまま胴体とし、その上に小ぶりな仏頭を載せた極めて特異な姿で、胴体には阿弥陀如来の坐像と密教的な文様が浅く刻まれる。1660年（万治3年）の造立と伝わり、銘の年号から「万治の石仏」と呼ばれる。丸い巨石の量塊感とちょこんと載った頭部の不均衡が独特の存在感を生み、芸術家・岡本太郎が1974年に「こんなに面白いものは見たことがない」と絶賛したことで全国的に有名になった。下諏訪町指定有形文化財。願い事を唱えながら石仏の周りを回る独特の参拝作法も親しまれている。
highlights: 巨大な自然石の胴体に小さな仏頭を載せた、量塊と頭部の不均衡が生む異形のシルエット
胴体に浅く刻まれた阿弥陀如来坐像と卍・日月・雲などの密教的文様
願い事を唱えながら石仏の周囲を3周する独特の参拝作法
from_kameyama: 車で約3時間30分（東名阪・中央道経由、岡谷IC／諏訪ICから約20〜30分）。鉄道ならJR中央本線「下諏訪駅」下車徒歩約30分。
summary_en: Across a small river from the spring shrine of Suwa Taisha sits one of Japan's most disarming pieces of folk sculpture: a Buddha that is essentially a boulder wearing a face. In 1657, the story goes, a stonemason set his chisel to a great block of andesite to carve a torii gate — and the stone bled. Spooked, the masons used a different stone and, in penance, turned this one into a seated Amida Buddha instead, finishing it in 1660, the third year of the Manji era, which is how it got its name. They barely touched it. The rounded natural mass of the rock was left as the body, incised only shallowly with a meditating Amida and a scatter of esoteric symbols — a swastika, sun, moon, clouds, lightning. Onto this enormous lump they perched a small, almost comically modest head. The result is a silhouette that shouldn't work and absolutely does: top-heavy in reverse, solemn and slightly funny at the same time. For three centuries it was just a beloved village 'Amida-sama' until 1974, when the artist Taro Okamoto stood in front of it and declared it the most fascinating thing he'd seen anywhere in the world. His enthusiasm — echoed by the novelist Jiro Nitta — made the anonymous wayside Buddha nationally famous overnight. Pilgrims still circle it three times, chanting a wish; it remains a town cultural property and a quietly strange object of real devotion.
highlights_en: A vast natural boulder left as the body, crowned by a tiny Buddha head — a top-heavy-in-reverse silhouette that is solemn and faintly comic at once
A shallowly carved seated Amida with esoteric symbols — swastika, sun and moon, clouds — incised over the robe of the raw stone
A distinctive worship rite of circling the Buddha three times while chanting one's wish

name: 善久寺（両面宿儺像）
name_kana: ぜんきゅうじ（りょうめんすくなぞう）
address: 〒506-2256 岐阜県高山市丹生川町日面788
fee: 拝観志納（御朱印300円）。本堂内拝観は要事前連絡
hours: 要事前連絡（御朱印は寺務所前の箱で随時）
summary: 飛騨・高山市丹生川町日面にある曹洞宗の寺院で、山号は普門山、飛騨三十三観音31番札所。境内には飛騨の異形伝説で知られる「両面宿儺」に因んだ両面宿儺像と十一面観音像が祀られ、いずれも高山市の指定文化財となっている。両面宿儺は一つの胴体に前後二つの顔と四本（八本とも）の手足を持つとされる伝承上の存在で、『日本書紀』では朝廷に討たれた賊として、飛騨の在地伝承では毒龍を退治し寺を開いた英雄・豪族として、対照的に語られる。善久寺はその両面宿儺が建立したとも伝わり、食事をとったという「御前石」も残る。円空作の迦楼羅像など文化財も伝わる、飛騨の民俗信仰を体現する寺。
highlights: 前後二つの顔と四本の手足を持つ「両面宿儺」を造形化した珍しい仏像（高山市指定文化財）
賊（日本書紀）と英雄（飛騨伝承）という両義的存在を祀る、民俗的に異色の信仰対象
両面宿儺が食事をとったと伝わる「御前石」など、伝説の痕跡が境内に残る
from_kameyama: 車で約3時間（東海北陸道・中部縦貫道経由で高山ICへ→丹生川方面）。日帰り可能だが余裕を持つと安心。
summary_en: Deep in the mountains of Hida, in the Nyūkawa district of Takayama, stands a small Sōtō Zen temple dedicated to one of Japan's strangest legendary beings. Ryōmen Sukuna — "the double-faced Sukuna" — is described in the eighth-century Nihon Shoki as a monstrous figure with a single body, two faces pointing in opposite directions, and four arms, who plundered the people of Hida until a court general struck him down. But the official chronicle is only half the story. In Hida's own oral tradition, the very same being is a hero: a local chieftain who slew a venomous dragon, protected the region from central authority, and founded temples — Zenkyū-ji among them. The temple keeps a carved statue of Ryōmen Sukuna and an Eleven-Faced Kannon, both designated cultural properties, along with a stone called the Gozen-ishi where he is said to have eaten, and a Garuda figure by the wandering monk-sculptor Enkū. To stand before the two-faced image is to stand inside an unresolved argument that is over a thousand years old: was he demon or savior? The villagers of Hida have always known their own answer. (Visiting the statue inside the hall requires contacting the temple in advance.)
highlights_en: A rare Buddhist statue depicting Ryōmen Sukuna with two faces and four limbs (a designated cultural property of Takayama City)
An unusual object of folk devotion: a being cast simultaneously as villain (Nihon Shoki) and hero (Hida tradition)
Traces of the legend remain on the grounds, including the Gozen-ishi stone where Ryōmen Sukuna is said to have eaten

name: 命と性ミュージアム女神館
name_kana: いのちとせいみゅーじあむめがみかん
address: 〒370-3606 群馬県北群馬郡吉岡町上野田1256-72
fee: 大学生・18歳以上 1,000円／中高生 800円／小学生以下 無料
summary: 群馬県吉岡町にある、人間の生命の始まりと性をテーマにした成人向けの私設博物館。2002年に開館し、前身は「伊香保女神館」。受胎から誕生に至る生命の仕組みや人体の構造、性を文化現象として扱う展示を、模型・パネル・造形物で時系列に沿って紹介する。性教育・性文化を正面から扱う展示内容から、教育学・社会学・地理学などの分野で大学の研究対象として取り上げられた例もある。一方で露骨な造形やユニークな世界観から、いわゆる「珍スポット」「秘宝館系」の博物館としても知られ、訪問記が数多く残る。成人向け展示を含むため中高生以下は内容に配慮が必要で、入館時の年齢区分が設けられている。関越自動車道渋川伊香保ICから車で十数分とアクセスは比較的容易。
highlights: 受胎から誕生までを時系列でたどる、生命の始まりと性をテーマにした大型造形・模型群
性教育・性文化を正面から扱い大学研究の対象にもなった教育的側面と珍スポット性の同居
前身「伊香保女神館」から続く、独特の世界観を持つ私設博物館の造形演出
from_kameyama: 車で片道約5時間半（亀山→東名・新東名→圏央道→関越道渋川伊香保IC→約13分）。日帰りは可能だが長距離。
summary_en: Tucked in the hills near Ikaho Onsen in Gunma, this private museum — opened in 2002 and formerly the Ikaho Goddess Hall — takes on the entire arc of human life and sexuality, from conception to birth, through models, panels, and figures arranged in chronological order. It belongs to the lineage of Japan's now-vanishing "hihokan" treasure halls and sex museums, but it sits awkwardly and interestingly between categories: part frank sex-education exhibit, part folk-curio sideshow. The first floor leans educational, walking visitors through human anatomy, the origins of life, conception, pregnancy, childbirth, and genetics; elsewhere the displays turn explicit and surreal in the distinctive register of the genre. That dual character has earned it a paradoxical reputation — cited as a research subject by university scholars in education, sociology, and geography, while also remaining one of Gunma's most reliably documented "strange spots." Admission is age-restricted, and the museum is best approached with that context in mind. Just over ten minutes by car from the Shibukawa-Ikaho expressway interchange, it is an easy add-on to a hot-spring trip.
highlights_en: Large figures and models tracing the beginning of life from conception to birth, themed on life and sexuality
A rare survivor of Japan's hihokan tradition that is both an explicit oddity and a cited academic subject in education and sociology
The distinctive worldview and figural staging carried over from its predecessor, the Ikaho Goddess Hall

name: まぼろし博覧会
name_kana: まぼろしはくらんかい
address: 〒413-0231 静岡県伊東市富戸梅木平1310-1
fee: 大人1,400円・小中学生600円（2024年改定）
summary: 静岡県伊東市富戸の国道135号沿いに立つ、出版社データハウスが運営する私設テーマパーク。閉園した熱帯植物園跡を約1億5000万円かけて改装し、2011年（平成23年）7月に開館した。館長は「セーラちゃん」を名乗る鵜野義嗣で、館内には巨大な聖徳太子像、ピラミッドやモアイなどの古代文明レプリカ、昭和の玩具・人形・ポスターを集めた懐古空間、剥製や秘宝館由来のオブジェを並べた魔界神社など、脈絡のない展示が密林状に詰め込まれている。「説明をあえて付けない」館長の方針のもと、来館者は意味づけの手前で異物の群れに対峙する。B級・珍スポットとしては国内最大級と評されるカオス空間である。
highlights: 高さ十数メートルの巨大聖徳太子像をはじめ脈絡なく林立する巨像・古代文明レプリカ
昭和の玩具・人形・ポスターと秘宝館由来のオブジェが密林状に詰め込まれた展示密度
「説明を付けない」館長セーラちゃんの方針が生む、意味づけを拒む独特の鑑賞体験
from_kameyama: 車で約4時間（新名神・新東名→東名・伊豆スカイライン等で伊東方面）。鉄道なら約4〜5時間（亀山→名古屋→新幹線で熱海、伊東線で伊東駅、バス約30分）。
summary_en: Along Route 135 on the Izu Peninsula, inside the cavernous greenhouses of a long-dead tropical botanical garden, a publisher decided to build a museum to nothing in particular — and that is exactly the point. Maboroshi Hakurankai, the 'Phantom Exposition,' opened in 2011 after Data House bought the abandoned park and spent some 150 million yen turning it into one of Japan's largest and most deliberately incoherent roadside attractions. Its director, a sailor-suited persona called 'Sera-chan' (the company president, Yoshitsugu Uno), follows a single rule: explain nothing. So you walk through a jungle where a giant statue of Prince Shōtoku looms over pyramid and moai replicas; through a Shōwa-era memory chamber packed with old toys, dolls and posters; through 'Makai Shrine,' a dim warren of taxidermy and unsettling figures; and past erotic objects salvaged from a defunct 'secret treasure hall.' Nothing connects, and nothing is captioned. Rather than horror, the experience is closer to outsider art — a place built outside every institution, where the sediment of postwar pop culture piles up at the scale of a temple and hands the job of making meaning back to you. Photography is free; bewilderment is included.
highlights_en: A jungle of unconnected colossi — a towering Prince Shōtoku statue, pyramid and moai replicas — looming with no captions to explain them
An overwhelming density of Shōwa toys, dolls and posters alongside objects from a former 'secret treasure hall'
Director Sera-chan's 'no explanation' policy, producing a viewing experience that actively refuses meaning

name: 南蔵院（釈迦涅槃像）
name_kana: なんぞういん（しゃかねはんぞう）
address: 〒811-2405 福岡県糟屋郡篠栗町大字篠栗1035
fee: 境内拝観無料。涅槃像の胎内参拝は志納（500円程度）
summary: 福岡県篠栗町の高野山真言宗・南蔵院に横たわる、全長41メートル・高さ11メートル・重さ約300トンの釈迦涅槃像。ブロンズ製の涅槃像としては世界最大級とされる。南蔵院は篠栗四国八十八箇所霊場の総本寺で、長年ミャンマーやネパールの子どもたちへ医薬品や文房具を支援してきた縁から、1988年にミャンマー仏教会議より仏舎利の贈呈を受けた。この三尊仏舎利を安置する場として、1992年から約3年をかけ1995年に涅槃像が建立された。横臥する巨大ブロンズ仏という造形の異様さに加え、住職が宝くじで1億3000万円の高額当選を重ねたという逸話から「金運の寺」としても広く知られる。
highlights: 全長41m・高さ11m・重さ約300トン、ブロンズ製涅槃像として世界最大級の横臥像
ミャンマーから贈られた仏舎利を安置するために1995年に建立された建立背景
胎内参拝でき、住職の宝くじ高額当選逸話から『金運の寺』として知られる文脈
from_kameyama: 亀山市からは空路が現実的。中部国際空港(セントレア)から福岡空港へ約1時間20分、福岡空港→（地下鉄・JR）篠栗線で「城戸南蔵院前駅」まで約40分、駅からすぐ。空港アクセス込みで片道半日程度。
summary_en: In the wooded hills of Sasaguri, just east of Fukuoka, a bronze Buddha 41 meters long lies on its side — one of the largest reclining Buddhas of its kind in the world, roughly the length of a jumbo jet and weighing some 300 tons. Reclining Buddhas of this scale belong to Thailand and Myanmar, not Japan, and that is precisely the point: Nanzoin's giant exists because of a connection to Myanmar. For years the temple shipped medicine and school supplies to children in Myanmar and Nepal, and in 1988 Myanmar's Buddhist conference repaid the kindness with a gift of sacred relics. The statue, completed in 1995 after three years of work, was built to house them. Nanzoin is also the head temple of the Sasaguri 88-temple pilgrimage, a serious place of faith threaded with Buddhist statuary, a Seven Lucky Gods tunnel, and sand-treading rites. And yet it is best known to many for a single irresistible rumor: its head priest is said to have won the lottery again and again, once to the tune of 130 million yen, making this colossal reclining Buddha an unlikely magnet for visitors praying for a windfall. We present the relic history as documented fact and the fortune legend as exactly that — legend.
highlights_en: A bronze reclining Buddha 41 m long, 11 m high and roughly 300 tons — one of the largest of its kind in the world
Built in 1995 to enshrine sacred relics gifted by Myanmar in thanks for the temple's aid to children there
Visitors can enter the statue's interior, and the priest's lottery-win legend has made it a famous 'fortune' temple

name: 日輪寺（山鹿大仏・おびんずる様）
name_kana: にちりんじ（やまがだいぶつ・おびんずるさま）
fee: おびんずる様拝観 大人約250円（寺院境内・つつじ公園は無料）
hours: 日中（つつじ公園は常時開放）
summary: 熊本県山鹿市の曹洞宗寺院・日輪寺の裏山に立つ巨大仏「おびんずる様（山鹿大仏）」。釈迦の弟子・賓頭盧尊者（びんずる）を、世界に類のない半臥（上を向いて寝た姿）で表した撫で仏で、高さ約30メートル・幅約10メートルにおよぶ。山の斜面に背を預けて全身を斜めに反らせた独特の姿勢から、巨大仏ファンの間で「ロボットのよう」とも評される。像の胸内には御本尊と、寝姿の分身の撫で仏が安置され、自分の患部と像の同じ部分を撫でると病が癒えると伝わる。寺は平安期に天台宗で開かれ鎌倉期に菊池氏が曹洞宗として再興、赤穂義士の遺髪塔やツツジの名所としても知られる。
highlights: 高さ約30mの巨大な「おびんずる様」が山の斜面に半臥（寝そべる）で寄りかかる唯一無二の姿勢
撫で仏（賓頭盧）信仰——自分の患部と像の同じ部位を撫でて病気平癒を願う
巨大仏でありながら境内に赤穂義士遺髪塔やツツジ園が同居する重層的な寺景
from_kameyama: 車で約8時間（名神・山陽道・九州道経由、植木IC／菊水ICから約30分）。現実的には飛行機（中部国際空港→福岡/熊本空港）＋レンタカーが一般的。
summary_en: On the wooded hillside behind a quiet Soto Zen temple in Yamaga, Kumamoto, a roughly 30-meter giant Buddha lies sprawled against the slope like nothing else in Japan. This is Obinzuru-sama — Pindola Bharadvaja, one of the Buddha's ten great disciples — built not seated, not standing, but in a half-reclining, face-up posture its makers describe as 'without parallel in the world.' The effect is genuinely uncanny: a vast figure leaning back into the mountain at an angle, so unexpectedly tilted that giant-Buddha enthusiasts have compared it to a reclining robot. There is sincere devotion underneath the spectacle. Pindola is Japan's classic nade-botoke, the 'rubbing Buddha': you stroke the statue on the same spot where your own body aches, and pray for it to heal. Inside the colossus's chest are enshrined the temple's principal image and a reclining double of the rubbing Buddha. The temple itself is no novelty — founded as a Tendai temple in the Heian period and revived as a Zen temple by the Kikuchi clan in 1316, it is famous for 200 cherry trees, 35,000 azaleas, a hair-relic pagoda of the Ako loyal retainers, and a gate bell counted among Higo's three great temple bells. The giant Obinzuru is the strangest thing on the grounds, but it sits inside a layered, working sacred landscape rather than apart from it.
highlights_en: An approximately 30 m giant 'Obinzuru-sama' reclining face-up against the mountainside — a posture its makers call unparalleled in the world
The nade-botoke (Pindola) practice: rub the statue where your own body hurts and pray for healing
A layered temple landscape where the giant Buddha coexists with an Ako-retainers hair-relic pagoda and a vast azalea garden

name: 大弁財天（最福寺）
name_kana: だいべんざいてん
address: 〒891-0133 鹿児島県鹿児島市平川町4850-1
fee: 拝観無料（公式に拝観料の記載なし）
hours: 拝観時間内（公式サイトに明記なし・要問い合わせ）
summary: 鹿児島市平川町の高野山真言宗・総本山烏帽子山最福寺の四恩殿に安置される、像高18.5メートルの木彫の弁財天坐像。「世界一の木彫弁財天」「世界最大の木彫座佛大弁財天」とされ、京都の仏師・松本明慶（松本工房）が約10年以上をかけて制作し、2000年（平成12年）5月14日に開眼した。寄木造で檜材を用い、総重量は100トン以上にのぼる。これを収める四恩殿は高さ約33メートル・幅約31.5メートル・奥行き約44メートルという巨大な堂で、屋内に18.5メートルの木彫像が座する規模感は他に類を見ない。現代に新造された木彫巨像という点で、伝統技術と現代の発願が結びついた稀有な造形であり、平成期を代表する宗教的モニュメントのひとつといえる。
highlights: 屋内に座する像高18.5メートルの「世界一の木彫弁財天」という圧倒的スケール
仏師・松本明慶が10年以上かけて寄木造・檜材で新造した平成の巨大木彫像
高さ33メートル級の四恩殿に巨像が収まる、堂と像の規模感の異様さ
from_kameyama: 車＋新幹線で片道約5〜6時間（亀山→名古屋→新幹線鹿児島中央駅→車で約30分）。日帰りは厳しく1泊推奨。
summary_en: Inside an immense hall in southern Kagoshima sits one of Japan's most improbable colossi: a wooden Benzaiten, 18.5 meters tall, carved entirely from cypress and weighing over 100 tons — billed as the largest wooden carved Benzaiten in the world. Most of Japan's giant Buddhas are cast bronze and centuries old; this one is neither. It is the work of the contemporary Kyoto master sculptor Matsumoto Myokei, who spent more than a decade joining wood into a seated goddess of water, music, eloquence, and wealth, finally consecrated on May 14, 2000. The Shion-den hall built to contain it is itself enormous — roughly 33 meters tall — and the sheer mismatch of scale, a wooden idol the height of a six-story building seated indoors, is what makes the encounter so disorienting. Benzaiten descends from the Hindu goddess Saraswati and merged in Japan with the deity Ugajin as Uga-Benzaiten; here that ancient lineage is rendered in fresh timber by a living workshop, a rare meeting of traditional carving technique and modern devotional ambition, and a signature religious monument of the Heisei era.
highlights_en: An 18.5-meter seated 'world's largest wooden Benzaiten' enshrined indoors — an overwhelming sense of scale
A Heisei-era colossus newly carved in joined cypress over more than a decade by master sculptor Matsumoto Myokei
The uncanny scale of a giant statue housed within the 33-meter Shion-den hall

name: 新北神社（石神さん・桃玉）
name_kana: にきたじんじゃ
address: 〒840-2102 佐賀県佐賀市諸富町大字為重1080
fee: 境内自由（参拝無料）。桃玉は社務所にて授与（初穂料の目安1個300円程度）。
hours: 境内自由・通年・無休（御祈祷・授与の対応は社務所受付時間内。事前予約推奨。TEL 0952-47-4848）
summary: 佐賀市諸富町為重に鎮座する古社で、素盞嗚尊を主祭神とし、用明天皇期（586年）の創建と伝える。境内最大の見どころは樹齢約2200年と伝わる御神木ビャクシン（イブキ）で、不老不死の仙薬を求めて来日した徐福が上陸の証に植えたとの伝説をもち、横へ大きく傾いだ幹を支柱で支える姿から「飛龍木」と呼ばれ、県の天然記念物・名木100選に選ばれている。傍らには樹齢約1600年のクスノキも立つ。本殿背後には宮地嶽神社・恵比寿社・稲荷社・淀姫神社など多数の摂末社と石祠群が並び、その一角に「石神さん」と呼ばれる御神体石が祀られる。厄除・八難祓い・縁結び・安産を願意とする祈祷で知られ、桃を厄祓いの呪具とみなす民俗（古事記の桃の魔除け伝承に連なる）に基づいた「桃玉」を石神さんに投じて願掛けする慣習が伝わる。秋の御神幸祭で奉納される三段継の三重の獅子舞は佐賀県重要無形文化財。
highlights: 横倒しに近い角度で這う幹を木の支柱で支える、樹齢約2200年の御神木ビャクシン『飛龍木』の異様な造形
本殿背後にひしめく石祠群と、その中心に祀られた御神体石『石神さん』
桃を魔除けの呪具とする民俗に基づき、石神さんへ『桃玉』を投じて願掛けする独特の作法
樹齢約1600年のクスノキの巨木が御神木のすぐ近くに並び立つ
県重要無形文化財・三重の獅子舞（曲芸的な三段継ぎ）が秋の大祭で奉納される
from_kameyama: 車で約7時間（名阪国道→新名神・名神→中国道・山陽道→九州道・有明海沿岸道路、諸富ICから約4分／約650km）。鉄道では亀山駅→名古屋駅（JR関西本線・快速で約1時間）→東海道・山陽・九州新幹線で博多経由→佐賀駅（合計約5時間半）、佐賀駅から諸富方面はバス・タクシーで約20〜25分。
summary_en: Nikita Shrine, an ancient shrine in the Morodomi district of Saga City, enshrines Susanoo-no-Mikoto and claims a founding in 586 CE. Its great oddity is the living one: a Chinese juniper said to be roughly 2,200 years old, planted — so legend holds — by Xu Fu, the envoy who sailed to Japan seeking the elixir of immortality and used the tree to mark his landing. The trunk no longer rises; it lunges almost sideways across the earth, propped up on wooden crutches, which is why it is called the "flying-dragon tree." A vermilion placard reading 飛龍木 leans against its base. Beside it stands a 1,600-year-old camphor, and behind the main hall crowds a dense field of small stone shrines, among them a sacred stone known simply as Ishigami-san, "the stone deity." Here the shrine's specialty — purification against all calamity — takes a physical, almost playful form: visitors receive a peach-shaped ball at the shrine office and throw it at the sacred stone to drive off misfortune and fix a wish. The gesture descends from one of Japan's oldest apotropaic images, the Kojiki episode in which the god Izanagi hurls peaches to repel the dead. Giant-tree worship, stone-deity worship, and the magic of the peach all settle into a single precinct, capped each autumn by the acrobatic, three-tier Mie lion dance, a designated prefectural cultural property.
highlights_en: A roughly 2,200-year-old sacred juniper whose near-horizontal trunk creeps across the ground on wooden supports — the so-called 'flying-dragon tree'
A dense field of stone shrines behind the main hall, centered on the sacred stone Ishigami-san
The distinctive rite of hurling a 'peach ball' at the stone deity, rooted in the folklore of the peach as a charm against evil
A roughly 1,600-year-old camphor tree standing right beside the sacred juniper
The acrobatic three-tier Mie lion dance, a Saga prefectural Important Intangible Cultural Property, offered at the autumn grand festival

name: ゴトゴト石
name_kana: ゴトゴトいし
address: 〒781-3221 高知県高知市土佐山桑尾
status: 現存（2022年11月のいたずら以降は揺れない状態）
fee: 無料
summary: 高知市土佐山桑尾の杉木立に鎮座する、高さ約1.5〜2m・重さ数トンとされる巨石。下の台石の上に微妙な均衡で乗っており、手で押すと「ゴトゴト」と音を立てて大きく揺れるのに、落ちそうで決して落ちないという奇石として知られる。子どもの力でも揺らせるほど敏感に動くが転倒しない不安定均衡の妙から、「落ちない＝合格する・当選する」と結びつけられ、受験生や選挙を控えた政治家が祈願に訪れる信仰の対象となった。地元桑尾地区では古くから守り神として親しまれ、しめ縄が掛けられ案内板も設置されている。旧土佐山村の広報誌に記載が現れるのは1984年で、それ以前の由来や設置経緯は役場にも記録がなく謎とされる。2022年11月のいたずら行為により現在は揺れなくなっているが、巨石と祈願の場としては変わらず見学できる。
highlights: 数トンの巨石が台石の上に絶妙な均衡で乗り、しめ縄を掛けられて杉林に鎮座する異様な造形
子どもでも揺らせるほど敏感に動くのに決して落ちないという不安定均衡の奇観（本来の状態）
「落ちない石」の語呂から受験合格・選挙当選を願う絵馬・祈願が集まる民間信仰の現場
from_kameyama: 車で約3時間40分（亀山ICから新名神・伊勢湾岸道・神戸淡路鳴門道・徳島道・高知道経由で高知ICまで約3時間、高知ICから県道44・16・33号で土佐山方面へ約40分の山道）。四国・高知の山間部で亀山からはかなり遠い。
summary_en: Deep in a cedar grove in the mountains of Kuwao, Tosayama, Kochi City, a boulder roughly 1.5 to 2 meters tall and said to weigh several tons rests on a base rock through a single delicate point of contact. Push it and the multi-ton mass rocks back and forth with an audible "goto-goto," tilting as if it must surely topple — and yet it never falls. A child can set it swaying, but nothing can bring it down. From that marvel of unstable equilibrium grew a peculiar folk belief: because the stone "never falls," exam candidates come to pray that they will not "fall" their tests, and politicians facing elections come to pray that they will win. The local Kuwao district has long cherished it as a guardian deity, draping it with a sacred straw rope (shimenawa) and tending an information board beside it. The oldest record of its existence is a 1984 village newsletter; how and why such a stone came to balance there is unknown even to the town office, and remains a genuine mystery. In November 2022 an act of vandalism left the stone unable to rock, and it has stood still ever since — but as a great balancing boulder and a place of prayer, it can still be freely visited.
highlights_en: A several-ton boulder balanced on a base rock at an improbable angle, draped with a sacred shimenawa rope and standing in a quiet cedar forest — an uncanny natural sculpture
The marvel of unstable equilibrium: a stone so sensitive that a child could rock it, yet so balanced it would never topple (in its original state)
A living site of folk belief, where the pun on the 'stone that never falls' draws prayers and votive offerings for passing exams and winning elections

name: 黒神埋没鳥居（腹五社神社）
name_kana: くろかみまいぼつとりい はらごしゃじんじゃ
address: 〒891-1401 鹿児島県鹿児島市黒神町647（黒神中学校横）
status: 現存（県指定天然記念物）
fee: 無料
hours: 常時見学可（屋外・無休）
summary: 大正3年（1914）の桜島大正噴火で、火山灰と軽石に埋もれた腹五社神社の鳥居。元は高さ約3mの石造鳥居だが、上部約1mを残してほぼ埋没した災害遺構。戦後、発掘せず『記憶の継承』のため現地保存され、1958年に鹿児島県天然記念物に指定された。
highlights: 笠木と貫だけを地表に残し、本来3mの鳥居がほぼ埋没した異様な姿
火山灰・軽石の堆積層に直接立つ、噴火災害の生々しい痕跡
発掘せず『記憶の継承』のためにあえて埋まったまま保存された判断
from_kameyama: 鉄道＋フェリーで現実的に1日がかり。新幹線で名古屋〜鹿児島中央（約3時間40分）、鹿児島港から桜島フェリー（約15分）、桜島港から車・路線バスで黒神まで約30分。
summary_en: On January 12, 1914, the great Taisho eruption of Sakurajima buried much of the village of Kurokami under pumice and ash within hours. Among the structures swallowed was the stone torii of Haragosha Shrine — originally about three meters tall, now reduced to roughly one meter of visible stone, its lintel and crossbeam resting almost at ground level atop a thick layer of solidified volcanic debris. After the war, residents began to dig it out, but the village head ordered the excavation stopped: the torii, he decided, should remain buried as a permanent reminder of the volcano's power. That choice turned a disaster ruin into one of Japan's earliest examples of deliberate in-place preservation. Designated a Natural Monument of Kagoshima Prefecture in 1958, the gate stands today beside Kurokami Junior High School, where it doubles as a disaster-education landmark. It is a quietly unsettling sight: the threshold to a sacred precinct, half-erased by the earth itself, on an island where the volcano is still very much alive.
highlights_en: A three-meter torii reduced to a one-meter stub — only the lintel and crossbeam remain above the hardened volcanic deposit
The gate stands directly on the 1914 ashfall layer, a raw and legible trace of the eruption
Deliberately left buried rather than excavated, as a chosen act of preserving disaster memory

name: 青峯山正福寺
name_kana: あおみねさん しょうふくじ
address: 三重県鳥羽市松尾町519
fee: 境内無料
hours: 境内自由（社務所・授与所は日中）
summary_en: Perched near the 336-meter summit of Mt. Aomine, this head temple of the Koyasan Shingon school enshrines an Eleven-faced Kannon and has, for centuries, drawn fervent devotion from fishermen and seafarers—not only around Ise-Shima and Ise Bay but from Hokkaido to Kyushu. A belief that "those who worship at Aomine are spared from storms" spread far and wide; legend tells of a golden Buddha that appeared riding on the back of a whale from the sea off Osatsu, with a "whale stone" said to be its petrified remains. The temple's great event is the Ofuna Matsuri on the 18th day of the first lunar month, when hundreds of brilliantly colored fishing flags dedicated by boats across Japan fill the precincts—a spectacle that turns a deep-mountain temple into something resembling a bustling harbor. Cultural relics such as the Great Gate (1830) and a seven-meter stone perpetual light (1837) attest to the prosperity of the shipping trade at its height.
highlights_en: At the Ofuna Matsuri, hundreds of fishing flags dedicated by boats nationwide fill the grounds in a riot of color
A maritime-faith sacred site that sits near a 336-meter mountain summit—an unexpected fusion of sea worship and deep-mountain temple
The legend of a golden Buddha that arrived on a whale's back, and the petrified "whale stone"
The Great Gate of 1830 and a roughly seven-meter perpetual light of the Tenpo era

name: 佐田西出 天王さんのお社塔
name_kana: さたにしで てんのうさんのおやしろとう
address: 三重県多気郡明和町大字佐田字西出
status: 現存（毎年再建・継承中）
fee: 無料（屋外・常時見学可）
summary: 明和町佐田の西出集落の路傍に立つ、麦藁と竹だけで毎年つくり替えられる小さな祠状の構造物。地元では牛頭天王を祀る「天王さん」と呼ばれ、津島神社の神札を内部に祀る。孟宗竹四本を約六十センチ間隔で地面に据えて柱とし、高さ約二メートル、その上に毎年新しく葺く麦藁屋根を載せる。屋根には三本の鰹木が表され、内部高さ一・三メートルほどに竹を組んだ棚を設けて祠とする。毎年七月十四日の天王祭を前に、集落の男性全員（約三十世帯・三十人）が前年のお社を見本に分解・複製して建て直す。屋根は毎年、竹の柱は二年ごとに新調される。常設の社殿ではなく、一年限りの素材で社を仮設し続けるという更新の作法そのものが信仰の核であり、平成三十一年（二〇一九）に明和町の無形民俗文化財に指定された。
highlights: 麦藁と竹だけで組まれた高さ約2メートルの祠状構造物が、住宅地のT字路の突き当たりに忽然と立つ意外性
毎年葺き替えられる麦藁屋根に三本の鰹木が表され、簡素な素材で神社建築の意匠を写しとっている造形
前年のお社を分解して見本とし、集落の男性全員で複製・再建するという『一年限りの社』の更新作法
from_kameyama: 車で約45〜55分（名阪国道・伊勢自動車道経由で松阪・明和方面へ、佐田地区へ。最寄りは近鉄山田線で斎宮駅方面）
name_en: Sata Nishide Tennō-san no Oyashiro-tō (Tennō-sama's Shrine Tower)
summary_en: Standing by the roadside in the Nishide hamlet of Sata, Meiwa Town, is a small shrine-like structure remade each year entirely from wheat straw and bamboo. Locally called 'Tennō-san,' it enshrines a deity of pestilence-warding (Gozu Tennō) with a talisman from Tsushima Shrine kept inside. Four mōsō bamboo poles, set about 60 cm apart, serve as pillars; the structure stands roughly two meters tall, topped by a wheat-straw roof re-thatched every year and bearing three katsuogi (decorative log ornaments symbolizing shrine architecture). Inside, at about 1.3 meters' height, a shelf of woven bamboo forms the sanctuary. Each year before the Tennō Festival on July 14, all the men of the hamlet — about 30 households, 30 people — dismantle the previous year's structure to use as a model and rebuild it from scratch; the roof is renewed annually and the bamboo pillars every two years. Rather than a permanent shrine, what lies at the heart of the faith is this very act of perpetually re-erecting a shrine from one-year-only materials. It was designated an Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Meiwa Town in 2019.
highlights_en: A roughly two-meter shrine-like structure built only from wheat straw and bamboo, standing unexpectedly at the dead-end of a T-junction in a residential neighborhood
An annually re-thatched straw roof bearing three katsuogi — reproducing the motifs of shrine architecture from the simplest of materials
The renewal custom of a 'one-year-only shrine': dismantling the previous year's structure as a model and rebuilding it with all the men of the hamlet

name: 大石林山
name_kana: だいせきりんざん
address: 〒905-1422 沖縄県国頭郡国頭村宜名真1241
status: 現存（2024年12月「ASMUI Spiritual Hikes／アスムイハイクス」へ改称）
summary_en: At the very northern tip of Okinawa's main island, where the East China Sea meets a tangle of subtropical jungle, the ground itself begins to behave strangely. Limestone laid down some 250 million years ago has been dissolved by rain over unimaginable stretches of time into a forest of blade-like grey pinnacles — a karst landscape said to be the northernmost of its tropical kind anywhere on Earth. The rocks rise like petrified swords through the green canopy, and walking the trails among them feels less like a hike than a passage through deep geological time. But Daisekirinzan is more than rock. The whole area overlaps with Asumui, the sacred ground where, in Ryukyuan creation myth, the ancestral deity Amamikyo is said to have first descended to begin the making of the islands. Particular stones and ancient banyans here are objects of prayer, and the site sits squarely within Okinawa's living utaki tradition, in which the land itself is holy. The park lies within Yanbaru National Park and beside a World Natural Heritage zone, and in December 2024 it was renamed "ASMUI Spiritual Hikes" to foreground exactly this fusion of rare geology and sacred landscape.
highlights_en: A forest of sword-like grey limestone pinnacles rising out of the subtropical jungle — a karst landscape of startling scale
Geologically rare: regarded as the northernmost tropical/subtropical karst in the world
Overlapping the sacred site of Asumui, where the Ryukyuan creator deity Amamikyo is said to have first descended, the rocks themselves are objects of prayer
Giant banyans and cycad colonies coexisting with the karst, forming a distinctive subtropical flora

name: 猿橋
name_kana: さるはし
address: 〒409-0614 山梨県大月市猿橋町猿橋
fee: 無料（見学自由）
summary_en: Spanning the deep gorge of the Katsura River in Otsuki, Yamanashi, the Saruhashi is a bridge that does the seemingly impossible: it crosses more than thirty meters of empty air without a single pier touching the water below. The gorge is too deep for conventional supports, so its builders solved the problem from the banks inward — stacking timber cantilever beams, the hanegi, in four projecting layers out from the bedrock of each side until they nearly meet, then laying the deck across the gap. The result hangs some 31 meters above the river, about 30.9 meters long, and seen from below the layered beams fan out like a wooden staircase reaching into the void. It is counted as one of the "Three Unusual Bridges of Japan," alongside the Kintai Bridge and the Kazura Bridge, and was designated a National Place of Scenic Beauty in 1932. Legend credits its design to Shirako, an immigrant garden master who is said to have watched monkeys link their bodies to form a living bridge across the chasm — hence the name, which means "monkey bridge." Hiroshige and other ukiyo-e masters painted it, and for centuries it has been admired as both an engineering marvel and a scenic wonder.
highlights_en: No piers at all: four stacked layers of cantilever beams project from each bank to span a gorge over 30 m wide
Hanging roughly 31 m above the river — seen from below, the layered beams fan out like a wooden staircase into the void
One of the rare "Three Unusual Bridges of Japan," a landmark in the history of bridge engineering
A cultural depth reaching back to the Edo period, painted by Hiroshige and admired as both marvel and scenic spot

name: 忍野八海
name_kana: おしのはっかい
address: 〒401-0511 山梨県南都留郡忍野村忍草
status: 現存（国指定天然記念物・世界文化遺産構成資産）
fee: 無料（八海の散策は自由。底抜池のある『榛の木林資料館』のみ有料）
hours: 散策自由（資料館等は施設ごとに営業時間あり）
summary_en: At the northern foot of Mount Fuji, in the village of Oshino, water that fell as rain and snow on the great volcano resurfaces — but only after a journey of some twenty years filtering down through layer upon layer of lava. What emerges is water of almost unreal clarity, pooling into eight springs whose names carry their own histories: Deguchi, Okama, Sokonuke, Choshi, Waku, Nigori, Kagami, and Shobu. Peer into Waku-ike, the most famous, and the surface seems to vanish: you look straight down into a deep emerald shaft where a lava cave descends into the earth, the water so transparent it scarcely registers as water at all. These are not ordinary ponds. Designated a National Natural Monument in 1934 and inscribed in 2013 as part of the World Cultural Heritage of Mount Fuji, the eight springs were a sacred circuit where Fuji-ko pilgrims purified themselves before ascending the mountain. By tradition, the distant Deguchi-ike represents Polaris and the remaining seven the Big Dipper — a constellation laid out in water. Here the hydrology of Mount Fuji and centuries of mountain worship are fused into a single, luminous landscape.
highlights_en: Springwater filtered through Mount Fuji's lava: clarity so complete you can see straight to the bottom, glowing emerald
Looking into Waku-ike reveals a lava cave descending into the earth beneath the transparent surface
A constellation in water: distant Deguchi-ike as Polaris and the seven others as the Big Dipper, marking it as a sacred site
The context of the 'eight-springs circuit,' where Fuji-ko pilgrims purified themselves before climbing the sacred mountain

name: たつこ像
name_kana: たつこぞう
address: 〒014-1206 秋田県仙北市西木町西明寺字潟尻
fee: 無料（見学自由）
summary_en: On the western shore of Lake Tazawa — at 423.4 meters the deepest lake in Japan — a golden figure stands ankle-deep at the water's edge, gazing out over an expanse of impossibly blue water. This is the Tatsuko statue, cast in bronze by the sculptor Funakoshi Yasutake and erected in 1968. The gold is no mere flourish: Lake Tazawa's water is strongly acidic, and the figure was finished in gold leaf and lacquer to shield the bronze from corrosion — a practical decision that produced one of the most striking color contrasts in the Tohoku landscape, a gleaming golden body against deep cobalt blue. The statue gives form to a local legend. A young woman named Tatsuko, desperate to keep her beauty and youth forever, prayed for a hundred days and nights and was told to drink from a spring to the north; she drank, and drank, until she transformed into a great dragon and sank to the bottom of the lake to become its eternal master. The figure stands on a pedestal that juts slightly into the water, so that statue and lake seem to merge — a princess paused at the threshold between her human form and the depths that took her.
highlights_en: A full-length golden bronze figure blazing against the cobalt-blue lake — an intense color contrast
Set on a pedestal jutting into the water at the lake's edge, so that statue and lake appear as one
The gold is born of function: a gold-leaf-and-lacquer finish to resist the acidic lake water
A statue that gives form to the legend of Princess Tatsuko, who became the dragon-master of the lake

name: 会津さざえ堂（旧正宗寺三匝堂）
name_kana: あいづさざえどう
address: 〒965-0003 福島県会津若松市一箕町大字八幡字弁天下1404
status: 現存（国指定重要文化財）
fee: 大人400円／高校生300円／小中学生200円
summary_en: On the slopes of Mount Iimori in Aizuwakamatsu stands a building that looks as though it is mid-collapse, or mid-spin — a hexagonal wooden tower of three tiers, about 16.5 meters tall, its timberwork twisting upward like a conch shell. Built in 1796 and formally named Entsu Sansodo, it is universally known as Sazaedo, the "turban-shell hall," and its strangeness is not skin-deep. Inside is one of the rarest architectural feats in Japan: a double-helix ramp on which visitors going up and visitors coming down travel entirely separate spiral paths and never once meet. A worshipper enters, walks a continuous slope clockwise to the top, then descends a different slope without ever retracing a step or passing another pilgrim. The design had a devotional purpose — thirty-three Kannon images once lined the spiral, so that a single circuit counted as completing the entire Saigoku thirty-three-Kannon pilgrimage. Conceived by the priest Ikudo, this corkscrew of a building is a pre-modern wooden anticipation of the double-helix, and it was designated a National Important Cultural Property in 1995. Mount Iimori is also remembered as the place where the young Byakkotai warriors took their lives, lending the spiral hall a quiet weight beyond its architectural oddity.
highlights_en: A double-helix ramp on which ascending and descending visitors never cross — an exceptionally rare wooden structure worldwide
The twisted hexagonal three-tiered exterior itself suggests a turban shell (sazae), an arresting oddity
Designed as a pilgrimage device: a single circuit of the hall counted as a full thirty-three-Kannon pilgrimage
A distinctive, almost tilting silhouette and timberwork that give it a powerful visual presence

name: 道の駅大歩危（妖怪屋敷と石の博物館）
name_kana: みちのえきおおぼけ ようかいやしきといしのはくぶつかん
address: 〒779-5452 徳島県三好市山城町上名1553-1
fee: 大人600円・小中学生300円・幼児無料（妖怪屋敷と石の博物館はセット）
hours: 9:00〜17:00（入館は16:30まで）／3〜11月は無休、12〜2月は月曜休館（祝日の場合は翌日）
summary: 国の名勝・天然記念物に指定された大歩危峡をのぞむ国道32号沿いの道の駅に併設された、二つの異色ミュージアム。一階「妖怪屋敷」は、山城町に古くから伝わる「児啼爺（こなきじじい）」をはじめとする地元伝承の妖怪約70体を立体展示し、当地が「妖怪の里」を名乗る根拠となっている。二階「石の博物館」には天然記念物の含礫片岩のほか、火星由来とされる隕石、地球最古級の岩石、世界の貴石原石などが並ぶ。妖怪民俗と地質標本という脈絡の異なる二つを一棟に同居させた構成自体が珍しく、観光ドライブの途上で地域の口承文化に触れられる場として機能している。
highlights: 地元伝承の妖怪約70体を立体造形で並べた一階「妖怪屋敷」
児啼爺（こなきじじい）発祥地を掲げる山城町ならではの妖怪コレクション
妖怪民俗と火星隕石・地球最古級岩石を同じ建物で見せる脈絡のなさ
from_kameyama: 車で約3時間（名阪国道→新名神→徳島道井川池田ICから国道32号南下）。鉄道はJR関西本線・近鉄で名古屋経由、岡山から特急南風でJR大歩危駅下車、駅から車約10分。
name_en: Oboke Roadside Station (Yokai Yashiki and Museum of Stones)
summary_en: Deep in the mountainous heart of Shikoku, where the Yoshino River carves the steep Oboke Gorge, sits a roadside station hiding one of Japan's stranger pairings of exhibits. Climb to the first floor and you enter the Yokai Yashiki — a Monster House where some seventy folk demons from the surrounding Yamashiro district stand frozen in dim light: pot-bellied river imps, a skeletal apparition with a lolling red tongue, the crouching Konaki-jiji whose cry, legend says, drove travelers mad on these very mountain paths. This valley markets itself as a "village of yokai," and these figures are the proof: living oral tradition, made of plaster and shadow. Then, with no transition whatsoever, the second floor becomes the Museum of Stones — conglomerate schist designated a natural monument, a purported fragment of Mars, rocks counted among the oldest on Earth. Folklore and geology, the land's ghosts and the land's bones, sharing a single building beside a parking lot. It is the kind of place you stumble into between hairpin turns and never quite forget.
highlights_en: A first-floor Monster House displaying around seventy three-dimensional yokai handed down in local Yamashiro folklore
A yokai collection unique to Yamashiro, which claims to be a birthplace of the Konaki-jiji
The sheer non-sequitur of housing yokai folklore alongside a Mars meteorite and some of Earth's oldest rocks in one building

name: タウシュベツ川橋梁（めがね橋）
name_kana: たうしゅべつがわきょうりょう めがねばし
address: 北海道河東郡上士幌町字ぬかびら源泉郷（糠平湖内）
status: 現存（崩落進行中・季節により水没）
fee: 無料（展望台見学）／橋への接近はツアー・徒歩のみ
hours: 展望台は終日（冬期積雪・ヒグマ注意）
summary: 旧国鉄士幌線のコンクリート製アーチ橋で、1937年に完成、長さ約130メートル・11連アーチからなる。1955年、糠平ダム建設に伴う路線付け替えで廃止され、ダム湖（糠平湖）の中に取り残された。湖の水位が低い1月頃に氷上に姿を現し、6月頃から沈み始め、8〜10月には湖底に没するため「幻の橋」と呼ばれる。風のない晴天時に水面へ橋が映ると眼鏡のように見えることから「めがね橋」の別名をもつ。凍結融解の繰り返しと水圧で崩落が進み、刻一刻と姿を変える儚さそのものが見どころとなっている、北海道遺産の近代化遺産。
highlights: ダム湖に取り残された11連コンクリートアーチ橋
水位で見え隠れする「幻の橋」と水面に映る「めがね橋」の景
凍結融解と水圧で崩落が進み毎年姿を変える、消えゆく遺構の儚さ
from_kameyama: 車＋フェリーや空路で半日以上。現実的には鉄道・空路で帯広へ（名古屋→とかち帯広空港 約1時間40分）、帯広から車で約1時間20分（国道273号・ぬかびら源泉郷方面）。
summary_en: Stranded in the middle of a man-made lake in eastern Hokkaido lies a bridge that is slowly dissolving back into the earth. Built in 1937 as part of the old national railway's Shihoro Line, the Taushubetsu River Bridge is a graceful run of eleven concrete arches, about 130 meters long. When the line was rerouted for a dam in 1955, the bridge was simply abandoned to the reservoir that rose around it — and ever since it has performed an eerie annual disappearing act. In deep winter, when the dam is drawn down, it surfaces from the frozen lakebed; by June it begins to sink; and from late summer it vanishes completely beneath the water. Locals call it the "phantom bridge." On a still, clear day its reflection completes a perfect circle on the water, giving it its other name — the "eyeglass bridge." Every freeze-thaw cycle gnaws at the concrete: an earthquake in 2003 brought down part of a sidewall, and engineers warn it could collapse entirely at any time. To stand at the lookout and watch it is to watch a human structure being patiently un-made by ice, water, and time — a ruin whose whole beauty lies in the certainty of its disappearance.
highlights_en: Eleven concrete arches stranded in the middle of a dam reservoir
A 'phantom bridge' that appears and vanishes with the water level, and an 'eyeglass' reflection on still water
An ever-changing, collapsing relic — the poignancy of a structure being slowly reclaimed by nature

name: 鬼ノ城（鬼城山）
name_kana: きのじょう きのじょうざん
address: 〒719-1101 岡山県総社市奥坂
status: 現存（国史跡・西門等を復元整備）
fee: 無料（鬼城山ビジターセンター・見学とも無料）
hours: ビジターセンター9:00〜17:00（月曜・祝日の翌日・年末年始休）／城跡は終日見学可
summary: 総社平野の北にそびえる標高約400mの鬼城山の山頂部を、約2.8kmの城壁がぐるりと取り巻く古代山城。城門4か所と水門6か所を備え、発掘調査から7世紀後半の築造と推定されるが、『日本書紀』など正史に記録がなく、誰が何のために築いたのかが判然としない「謎の城」として知られる。最大の特色は、復元された壮大な西門と、白く版築された城壁が稜線に連なる景観で、古代山城でこれらの防御施設をまとまって復元している例は全国でも珍しい。地元では、百済の王子とされる鬼「温羅（うら）」が籠もり吉備津彦命に討たれたという伝説の舞台とされ、桃太郎伝説の源流に位置づけられる。
highlights: 稜線を約2.8kmの城壁が取り巻く古代山城の威容
版築土塁の上に復元された壮大な西門と眼下に広がるパノラマ
桃太郎伝説の源流とされる鬼『温羅（うら）』伝説の舞台
from_kameyama: 車で約2時間40分（名阪国道→新名神→山陽道、岡山総社IC経由）。鉄道はJRで岡山経由、JR吉備線「服部駅」からタクシー、または総社駅から車で麓へ。山上の西門まで駐車場から徒歩約15分。
summary_en: On a 400-meter peak above the Soja Plain stands a castle that history forgot to record. For some 2.8 kilometers, a band of rammed-earth wall and stone rings the summit of Mt. Kinojo, pierced by four great gates and six water gates — yet not a single line about its building survives in the Nihon Shoki or any other chronicle. Excavations point to the late 7th century, most likely raised by the Yamato court to guard against a feared invasion after Japan's catastrophic defeat at the Battle of Baekgang in 663. But it is the legend, not the archaeology, that gives the place its name and its chill: this was the lair of Ura, an ogre said to be a Baekje prince, who terrorized the land of Kibi until the hero Kibitsu-hiko cut him down — a story scholars read as the memory of Korean ironworking immigrants clashing with central power, and the very seed from which the tale of Momotaro grew. Today the reconstructed west gate rises white and stark on its rammed-earth platform, banners of red and white along its eaves, the plain spread out far below. Few ancient mountain castles in Japan let you walk this fully restored line of walls — and fewer still come haunted by an ogre.
highlights_en: The imposing sweep of an ancient mountain castle, walls ringing the ridge for some 2.8 km
A grand restored west gate atop a rammed-earth rampart, with a panorama over the plain below
The stage of the ogre 'Ura' legend, regarded as the source of the Momotaro tale

name: 赤神神社五社堂
name_kana: あかがみじんじゃごしゃどう
address: 〒010-0673 秋田県男鹿市船川港本山門前字祓川35
status: 現存（国指定重要文化財）
fee: 無料
hours: 境内自由（社務所・授与は不定。日没前の参拝・下山推奨）
summary: 男鹿半島・本山の中腹に鎮座する古社で、鬼が一夜で築いたと伝わる「九百九十九段の石段」の先に、横一列に並ぶ五棟の社殿（五社堂）が現れることで知られる。五社堂は正面入母屋造・背面切妻造の建築で、向かって右から三の宮堂・客人権現堂・赤神権現堂（中央）・八王子堂・十禅師堂と称し、1710年（宝永7年）の建立。五棟および中央堂内の厨子は国の重要文化財に指定されている。漢の武帝が連れてきた五匹の鬼の伝説は、男鹿のナマハゲ行事の起源譚のひとつとして語られ、円空作と伝わる十一面観音も祀られる。
highlights: 鬼が一夜で築いたと伝わる九百九十九段の苔むした石段
横一列に整然と並ぶ五棟の入母屋造社殿（1710年建立・重要文化財）
男鹿のナマハゲ起源譚と結びつく『五匹の鬼』の伝承
円空作と伝わる十一面観音を祀る神仏習合の聖地
from_kameyama: 車＋新幹線で約7〜8時間。亀山ICから名古屋経由、東北新幹線で秋田方面へ向かい、秋田駅・男鹿駅からレンタカーで本山中腹の駐車場へ。実質的には現地で車が必須の遠隔地。
name_en: Goshado of Akagami Shrine (The Five Halls)
summary_en: Climb 999 moss-slick stone steps up the side of Mt. Honzan on the Oga Peninsula, and the forest opens onto something improbable: five small wooden halls standing in a single perfect row, like a chorus line frozen on the mountainside. These are the Goshado of Akagami Shrine—five irimoya-roofed halls rebuilt in 1710, designated National Important Cultural Properties along with the late-Muromachi shrine cabinet kept inside the central hall. The staircase itself is the legend. Villagers, tormented by five demons brought to the peninsula by Emperor Wu of Han, wagered that if the demons could lay a thousand steps in one night they could take a village girl. At 999 steps a villager crowed like a rooster to fake the dawn, and the demons fled—one step short forever. Those same five demons are told as an origin of Oga's famous Namahage, the masked visiting deities. Inside, an Eleven-Faced Kannon attributed to the wandering monk-sculptor Enku ties the site to centuries of Buddhist–Shinto syncretism. The reward for the lung-burning climb is a row of weathered halls so orderly the press once called them 'an idol group lined up onstage.'
highlights_en: 999 moss-covered stone steps that demons supposedly built in a single night before a faked rooster's crow stopped them one step short
Five irimoya-roofed halls standing in a single neat row (built 1710; Important Cultural Properties)—a rare arrangement nationwide
Directly tied to the origin legend of Oga's Namahage visiting-deity ritual
An Eleven-Faced Kannon attributed to the monk-sculptor Enku, marking centuries of syncretism

name: 杉の大スギ（八坂神社）
name_kana: すぎのおおすぎ
address: 〒789-0311 高知県長岡郡大豊町杉794（八坂神社境内）
status: 現存（国の特別天然記念物）
fee: 見学200円程度（協力金）／境内自由
hours: 境内自由（日中の見学推奨）
summary: 高知県大豊町・八坂神社の境内に立つスギの巨木で、推定樹齢3000年以上、根元で「南大スギ」と「北大スギ」の二株に分かれることから「夫婦杉」とも呼ばれる。南大スギは樹高約60m・幹周約20m、北大スギは樹高約57m・幹周約16.5mに達し、神社境内の樹木としては日本最大とされる。1924年に国の天然記念物、1952年に特別天然記念物に指定された。1947年、療養中の9歳の美空ひばりが「日本一の歌手にしてください」と願をかけた逸話で広く知られる。
highlights: 根元で二株に分かれる『夫婦杉』、最大幹周約20m・樹高約60m
推定樹齢3000年超、神社の樹木として日本最大級の巨樹
美空ひばりが『日本一の歌手に』と願掛けした逸話
国の特別天然記念物に指定された巨樹信仰の象徴
from_kameyama: 車で約3.5〜4時間（亀山IC→新名神・神戸淡路鳴門・高松道経由、大豊ICから国道32号で約15分）。鉄道なら土讃線「大杉駅」下車、徒歩約25分。
name_en: Sugi no Osugi — The Great Cedar of Yasaka Shrine
summary_en: In a small mountain shrine in Kochi stands a cedar so old it predates almost everything human around it—estimated at more than three thousand years. Sugi no Osugi rises from a single base into two enormous trunks leaning toward each other, which earned it the name 'the husband-and-wife cedar.' The larger, southern trunk climbs to about 60 meters with a girth near 20 meters; together they form the largest tree found in any shrine precinct in Japan, designated a Special Natural Monument in 1952. Legend credits the storm god Susanoo with planting it, and a warrior is recorded enshrining three deities at its roots in the year 912. But the story that draws visitors today is more recent: in 1947 a nine-year-old girl injured in a traffic accident nearby came to pray here, asking the tree to make her the greatest singer in Japan. The girl was Misora Hibari, who became exactly that—and after her death the town built a memorial garden where her songs still play on the breeze beside the colossal trunks. Standing beneath two living things older than the country's written history, with a postwar icon's voice drifting through, is the particular strangeness of this place.
highlights_en: A single base splitting into two giant trunks—the 'husband-and-wife cedar'—the larger near 20 m girth and 60 m tall
Estimated at over 3,000 years old; the largest tree in any shrine precinct in Japan
The tree to which a nine-year-old Misora Hibari prayed to become 'the best singer in Japan'
A National Special Natural Monument and a living emblem of Japan's giant-tree worship

name: 沢田マンション
name_kana: さわだまんしょん
address: 〒780-0972 高知県高知市薊野北町一丁目10番3号
status: 現存（現役の集合住宅・居住中）
fee: 無料（外観・1階通路の見学のみ。住民の生活空間につき配慮必須）
hours: 見学時間の定めなし（常識的な日中・住民配慮の範囲で）
summary: 高知市にある、鉄骨鉄筋コンクリート造・地上5階（一部6階）の大規模集合住宅。建築の専門教育を受けていない沢田嘉農・裕江夫妻が、1971年の着工から家族総動員で独学・自力建設したセルフビルド建築として知られる。「設計図は頭の中にある」として図面を持たず増改築を重ねた結果、迷路のように入り組んだ外観となり、「日本の九龍城」「軍艦島マンション」「日本のサグラダ・ファミリア」とも呼ばれる。屋上には自作クレーンや畑、4階には池、地下には駐車場を備える。建築確認申請が出されておらず違法建築だが、現在も約70戸・約100人が暮らす現役物件。
highlights: 図面なしで増改築を重ねた迷路状の巨大セルフビルド外観
屋上の自作クレーン・畑、4階の池など破格の自作設備
『日本の九龍城』と称される独学建築の到達点
今も約100人が暮らす現役の違法建築という両義性
from_kameyama: 車で約4〜4.5時間（亀山IC→新名神・神戸淡路鳴門・高松道・高知道、高知ICから至近）。鉄道なら岡山経由・土讃線で高知へ、JR土讃線「薊野（あぞの）駅」下車。
summary_en: From across the street it looks like a piece of a city that drifted loose and ran aground—a sprawling, terraced concrete mass of balconies, ramps, ladders, and plants that has earned the nickname 'Japan's Kowloon Walled City.' Sawada Mansion is a five-story (part six) apartment block in Kochi, and its strangeness is entirely deliberate, and entirely amateur. Beginning in 1971, a couple with no architectural training—Kano and Hiroe Sawada—built the whole thing themselves, mixing and pouring concrete by hand, mobilizing their own family, and extending it piece by piece for over a decade. There were no blueprints. 'The plan is in my head,' Kano is quoted as saying, and the result is a glorious labyrinth: a homemade crane parked on the roof, vegetable plots and even rice grown up top, a fishing pond on the fourth floor, an underground garage below. Legally it is an illegal building—no construction permit was ever filed—but roughly a hundred people still live here, and the structure has become a pilgrimage site for architects and urban explorers alike, the ultimate monument to self-taught building. It is, crucially, a real home: visitors stick to the exterior and the ground-floor passage, and the residents' quiet lives come first.
highlights_en: A vast self-built labyrinth of an exterior, extended and rebuilt for over a decade with no blueprints
Astonishing homemade infrastructure: a rooftop crane and farm plots, a fishing pond on the fourth floor
Nicknamed 'Japan's Kowloon Walled City'—the high-water mark of self-taught architecture
An active, illegal building where about 100 people still live—its central contradiction

name: 押戸石の丘
name_kana: おしといしのおか
address: 〒869-2502 熊本県阿蘇郡南小国町中原
fee: 見学料あり（おおむね大人300円程度・受付にて）
hours: 受付制（日中。天候・季節で変動、事前確認推奨）
summary: 熊本県南小国町、阿蘇外輪山の標高約845mの草原の丘に大小数百個の石が点在し、その中で最大の巨石が通称「押戸石」と呼ばれる遺跡的景観地。頂上付近の巨石には線刻文様（ペトログラフ）があり、シュメール文字との関連やシュメール系海洋民族の祭祀の場であったとする説が唱えられてきた。山頂一帯の石群は人工的に配置された環状列石とする見解もある。巨石の一部では方位磁石が大きく振れる磁気異常が観察され、「太陽石」などの名で親しまれる。学術的評価には諸説があるが、阿蘇のカルデラと九重連山を望む360度の草原景観の中に巨石が立ち並ぶ独特の場である。
highlights: 標高約845mの草原に立ち並ぶ大小数百個の巨石群
巨石に刻まれた線刻文様（ペトログラフ）とシュメール文字説
方位磁石が大きく振れる磁気異常（『太陽石』など）
阿蘇カルデラと九重連山を望む360度の大草原パノラマ
from_kameyama: 車で約6時間（亀山IC→新名神・山陽道・九州道、熊本IC・大津から国道57・212号・マゼノミステリーロード経由）。空路なら阿蘇くまもと空港からレンタカーで約1.5時間。
summary_en: On a windswept grassland hill at 845 meters on the outer rim of the Aso caldera, several hundred stones lie scattered across an open ridge with a 360-degree view of volcanoes and rolling pasture—and at the center stand a handful of megaliths large enough to feel deliberate. This is Oshitoishi-no-Oka, one of Japan's most intriguing 'mystery' sites. Faint incised lines on the largest stones were examined decades ago, and the Japan Petroglyph Association proposed that they were ancient script related to Sumerian writing, with the hill once a ritual site for a Sumerian-linked seafaring people; others have argued the arrangement forms a prehistoric stone circle. None of this is settled archaeology—it belongs to the realm of theory and romance—but the strangeness is reinforced by something measurable: near a stone nicknamed the 'Sun Stone,' a magnetic anomaly is strong enough to send a compass needle spinning. Whether you read it as a prehistoric observatory, a power spot, or simply an astonishing place to stand, the hill delivers the same thing—megaliths silhouetted against the vast green caldera, where ancient mystery and pure scenery become hard to tell apart.
highlights_en: Several hundred megaliths scattered across an 845 m grassland hill
Incised line patterns (petroglyphs) on the stones and the contested theory linking them to Sumerian script
A magnetic anomaly that visibly swings a compass near the 'Sun Stone'
A full 360-degree panorama over the Aso caldera and the Kuju mountains

name: 通潤橋
name_kana: つうじゅんきょう
address: 熊本県上益城郡山都町長原
fee: 橋上見学（放水日のみ・有料）高校生以上500円／小中学生200円。橋を見るだけは無料
hours: 通潤橋ミエルテラス（物産館）9:00〜17:00。橋上開放は放水日の10:00〜15:00（観覧証販売は14:30まで）
summary_en: Built in 1854 on the orders of village headman Yasunosuke Futa to carry water to the parched Shiraito Plateau, Tsujun Bridge is a single-arch stone aqueduct about 78 meters long and 20 meters high, with three stone pipes running across its top that lift water uphill using the inverted-siphon principle. Its signature spectacle is the water discharge: to flush silt from the conduit, water blasts out from both sides of the arch in great fanning jets — a stone bridge that appears to spit waterfalls into the gorge below. In 2023 it became the first civil-engineering structure in Japan ever designated a National Treasure. What began as a working irrigation device — and a maintenance routine of clearing the pipes — has become a beloved icon, a place where Edo-period waterworks engineering meets the folk reverence for water that sustains a region's fields to this day.
highlights_en: Powerful fan-shaped water jets bursting from both sides of the stone arch (the silt-flushing discharge)
A massive single stone arch roughly 78 meters long and 20 meters high
Edo-period hydraulic engineering that carries water across a gorge using an inverted siphon
The first civil-engineering structure in Japan to be designated a National Treasure

name: 武雄の大楠
name_kana: たけおのおおくす
address: 佐賀県武雄市武雄町大字武雄5335 武雄神社
status: 現存（武雄市天然記念物）
fee: 無料（拝観自由）
hours: 見学自由（参道の開放時間に準じる。日中の明るい時間帯推奨）
summary_en: Hidden at the end of a bamboo path behind the main hall of Takeo Shrine in Saga Prefecture stands a colossal camphor tree said by tradition to be 3,000 years old. About 30 meters tall, 20 meters around the trunk, and 33 meters across its crown, it ranks among Japan's greatest trees. Its most striking feature is the cavernous hollow that gapes open at ground level: roughly the size of twelve tatami mats, with a small shrine to the deity Tenjin enshrined deep inside. The gnarled bark, likened to an elephant's legs, and the half-decayed yet still-living trunk embody the ancient folk reverence for giant trees as dwellings of the gods — a place where the boundary between living wood and sacred architecture dissolves. (Note: it is a Takeo City Natural Monument, not a national one, though the two are often confused.)
highlights_en: A roughly twelve-tatami hollow gaping open at ground level, with a Tenjin shrine enshrined deep within
A giant body about 30 meters tall and 20 meters around — among the largest trees in Japan
Gnarled bark resembling an elephant's legs and a trunk half-decayed and hollowed yet still alive
Folk veneration of a god-dwelling tree said to be 3,000 years old

name: 西の河原公園（賽の河原）
name_kana: さいのかわらこうえん
address: 群馬県吾妻郡草津町草津521-3
fee: 公園は無料（園内の西の河原露天風呂は別途有料）
hours: 公園は24時間開放（夜間は日没〜22:00ライトアップ）。露天風呂は別営業時間
summary_en: At the western edge of Kusatsu Onsen, hot spring water wells up not from a single source but from across an entire riverbed — about 1,400 liters a minute, some of it acidic enough (around pH 1.5) to rank among the most corrosive natural waters in Japan. That acidity kills almost all plant life, leaving a bleached, steaming wasteland of pale rock that early visitors compared to the Sai-no-Kawara, the Buddhist limbo where dead children pile stones beside the river of the underworld. The place was once called Oni no Senzui, the demon's spring waters, and legend held that shouting here would summon demons. A 15-minute walk from the Yubatake, the park is free to enter and contains one of Japan's largest open-air baths; at night it glows under lighting by the same designer who lit the Yubatake. It is a hot-spring hell made literal — where the blessing of the waters and a landscape that refuses life occupy the same ground.
highlights_en: Strongly acidic water welling up across the whole riverbed (~1,400 L/min), a desolate hellscape where nothing grows
Bleached rock and rising steam evoking the Buddhist underworld of the Sai-no-Kawara
The folklore of 'Oni no Senzui' and a legend that shouting summons demons
One of Japan's largest open-air baths and a fantastical night illumination

name: 寒風山回転展望台
name_kana: かんぷうざんかいてんてんぼうだい
address: 秋田県男鹿市脇本富永字寒風山1-1
fee: 大人540円、小中高生270円（団体15名以上：大人430円・小中高生220円）
hours: 8:30〜17:00（3月中旬〜12月上旬の期間営業／冬季は積雪のため閉鎖）
summary: 男鹿半島の付け根、標高約355mの寒風山頂に立つ円筒形の展望施設。最上階（4階）の展望フロアが床ごとゆっくり回転し、約13分で一周する間に、座ったまま大潟村・八郎潟・男鹿三山・鳥海山・日本海の360度パノラマが切り替わっていく仕組みである。寒風山自体が芝草に覆われたなだらかな草原状の火山で、樹木がほとんど無いため視界を遮るものがない。回転展望台は1964年の開業以来、昭和の観光開発期に各地へ建てられた「回転展望」の系譜を今に伝える数少ない現役施設のひとつであり、芝山の頂にUFOのような建屋がぽつんと載る光景そのものが珍スポット的な存在感を放っている。
highlights: 床が約13分で一周する回転展望フロア。座ったまま360度の景色が入れ替わる
樹木のない芝草の山頂に円筒形の建屋が単独で載る、UFO然とした外観
大潟村の干拓地・八郎潟・鳥海山・日本海を一望する遮蔽物のない大展望
昭和の観光開発が生んだ『回転展望台』様式の現役遺産
from_kameyama: 車＋公共交通で約9〜10時間（実質は飛行機利用が現実的）。鉄道なら名古屋から秋田新幹線・奥羽本線経由で秋田駅→JR男鹿線「男鹿駅」、駅からタクシーで約20分。車のみなら名阪国道・東名・北陸道・日本海東北道経由で約800km。
summary_en: At the base of the Oga Peninsula, on the grassy summit of Mount Kanpu (around 355 m), stands a lone cylindrical observation tower whose top floor literally turns. The 4th-floor deck rotates with its floor, completing one full revolution in about 13 minutes, so that a seated visitor watches a 360-degree panorama slide past — the great Ogata reclaimed plain, the Hachirogata basin, the Oga Sanzan peaks, distant Mount Chokai, and the Sea of Japan. Because Mount Kanpu is an almost treeless meadow volcano, nothing blocks the view. Opened in 1964, the observatory is one of the few survivors of the 'rotating observatory' fad that swept Showa-era Japan, and the sight of a UFO-like drum perched alone on a bald green hill gives the place a genuinely strange roadside-attraction presence.
highlights_en: A rotating observation floor that completes one revolution in about 13 minutes — the view changes around you while you sit still
A cylindrical building standing alone on a treeless grassy summit, looking almost like a landed UFO
An unobstructed grand panorama taking in the Ogata reclaimed farmland, Hachirogata, Mount Chokai, and the Sea of Japan
A still-operating relic of the Showa-era 'rotating observatory' style of tourism architecture

name: 龍河洞
name_kana: りゅうがどう
address: 高知県香美市土佐山田町逆川1424
fee: 観光コース 大人1,200円・中学生700円・小学生550円（冒険コース等は別料金・要予約）
summary: 日本三大鍾乳洞のひとつに数えられる高知県香美市の鍾乳洞で、1934年に国の天然記念物および国の史跡に重複指定された珍しい洞窟。約1kmの観光コースには石灰岩が長い年月をかけて造った石筍・石柱・流れるような石灰華段が連なる。最大の見どころは『神の壺』で、弥生時代後期に洞内へ持ち込まれた土器が、滴り落ちる石灰分に覆われて鍾乳石と完全に一体化したもの。約2000年をかけて土器が岩へと変わりつつある世界的にも稀な標本で、考古遺物と地質現象が同時に進行する稀有な証拠として知られる。観光コースのほか、ヘルメット着用で本来の入口を這い進む『冒険コース』も用意される。
highlights: 弥生土器が石灰華に覆われ鍾乳石と一体化した『神の壺』。約2000年かけ土器が岩に変わりつつある稀有な標本
天然記念物と史跡の両方に指定された、自然と人類史が交差する洞窟
石筍・石柱・流れるような石灰華段が約1km続く観光コース
ヘルメット必須で原始の入口を這い進む『冒険コース』
from_kameyama: 車で約4時間（名阪国道・大阪・神戸淡路鳴門道・高松道・高知道・国道経由で約330km）。鉄道なら岡山経由でJR土讃線「土佐山田駅」、駅からバスまたはタクシーで約20分。
summary_en: Counted among the 'Three Great Limestone Caves of Japan,' this Kochi cavern in Kami City was, unusually, designated both a National Natural Monument and a National Historic Site in 1934. Its roughly one-kilometer tourist route runs past stalagmites, stone pillars, and flowing flowstone terraces shaped over enormous spans of time. The headline marvel is the 'Vessel of the Gods' (Kami-no-tsubo): a clay pot carried into the cave in the late Yayoi period that, coated by dripping lime, has fused completely with the cave rock. Over some 2,000 years the pottery is literally turning to stone — a globally rare specimen in which an archaeological artifact and a geological process advance together. Beyond the sightseeing course, an 'adventure course' lets visitors crawl in through the cave's original natural mouth wearing a helmet.
highlights_en: The 'Vessel of the Gods' — a Yayoi pot wrapped in flowstone and fused with the cave rock, a rare specimen turning to stone over ~2,000 years
A cave designated as both a Natural Monument and a Historic Site, where nature and human history intersect
A tourist route of stalagmites, stone pillars, and flowing flowstone terraces running about one kilometer
An 'adventure course' crawling in through the primeval entrance with a helmet required

name: 池島炭鉱（廃坑ツアー）
name_kana: いけしまたんこう
address: 長崎県長崎市池島町1411-1
status: 現存（炭鉱体験施設は2027年3月末で廃止予定）
fee: 炭鉱体験ツアー 有料（コース別・予約制／別途フェリー運賃）
hours: ツアーは予約制（午前11:00〜・午後13:15〜等のコース制）。施設受付 8:30〜17:00。月曜・第2第4木曜・年末年始休
summary: 長崎市の外海（そとめ）沖に浮かぶ周囲約4kmの離島・池島にある炭鉱跡。戦後1952年から採炭が始まり、最盛期には約8000人が暮らした九州最後の炭鉱の島で、2001年に閉山した。現在は電動トロッコ（バッテリーカー）に乗り、元炭鉱マンのガイドに導かれて坑道へ入る『池島炭鉱体験ツアー』が行われており、稼働当時の機材を使って実際の採炭機械の模擬操作まで体験できる国内唯一の施設である。アパート群が廃墟化した島の景観は『第二の軍艦島』とも呼ばれるが、軍艦島と異なり今も約100人が暮らし、坑内に立ち入れる点が決定的に珍しい。なお炭鉱体験施設は2027年3月末で廃止予定。
highlights: 電動トロッコで実際の坑道へ入り、元炭鉱マンの案内で採炭機械を模擬操作できる国内唯一の体験
閉山した炭鉱の坑内にいまも立ち入れる稀有な近代化遺産
高層アパート群が廃墟化し『第二の軍艦島』と呼ばれる離島景観（ただし現役で人が住む）
九州最後の炭鉱の島という産業史的文脈
from_kameyama: 車＋フェリーで約9時間。新幹線なら名古屋→博多→長崎、長崎市から神浦港または瀬戸港へ路線バス、そこから池島行きフェリー（約30分前後）。離島アクセスのため終日行程。
summary_en: Off the Sotome coast of Nagasaki lies Ikeshima, a small island roughly 4 km around that was the last working coal-mine island in Kyushu. Mining began in 1952; at its peak some 8,000 people lived here, until the pit closed in 2001. Today visitors board an electric trolley (battery car) and, led by guides who are themselves former miners, ride into the actual tunnels — and can even simulate operating real coal-cutting machinery from the working era. It is the only facility of its kind in Japan. The island's ruined apartment blocks have earned it the nickname 'the second Battleship Island,' but unlike Hashima, about 100 people still live here and, crucially, you can go underground. Note that the coal-mine experience facility is scheduled to close at the end of March 2027.
highlights_en: The only experience in Japan where you ride an electric trolley into real tunnels and simulate operating mining machinery under a former miner's guidance
A rare modernization heritage where you can still enter the tunnels of a closed coal mine
An island landscape of ruined high-rise apartment blocks dubbed 'the second Battleship Island' — yet still inhabited
The industrial-history context of being Kyushu's last coal-mine island

name: 飯盛山さざえ堂（旧正宗寺三匝堂）
name_kana: いいもりやまさざえどう
address: 〒965-0003 福島県会津若松市一箕町大字八幡字弁天下1404-2
status: 現存（国指定重要文化財）
fee: 大人400円、大高生300円、小中生200円
summary: 寛政8年（1796）に飯盛山の正宗寺住職・僧郁堂が考案したとされる、高さ約16.5メートル・六角三層の木造仏堂。正式名称は円通三匝堂（えんつうさんそうどう）で、重要文化財指定名称は旧正宗寺三匝堂。最大の特徴は、内部が上りと下りで別経路となる二重らせん構造のスロープを採用している点で、参拝者は堂内を一巡しても他者とすれ違わずに昇降できる。
highlights: 上り・下りが完全に分離した木造の二重らせんスロープ構造（堂内ですれ違わない）
六角三層・高さ約16.5メートルの、ねじれて見える独特の外観
堂内を一巡するだけで西国三十三観音巡礼を疑似体験できる巡礼装置としての機能
from_kameyama: 車では遠方のため鉄道推奨。亀山駅→（関西本線・新幹線等）→郡山駅乗換→会津若松駅、所要約5〜6時間。会津若松駅からバス・タクシーで飯盛山下まで約15分。車なら名阪・新名神・東名・東北道経由で約9〜10時間。
summary_en: Rising 16.5 meters from a hillside in Aizu, this hexagonal three-story wooden hall — completed in 1796 and formally named Entsu Sansodo — looks from the outside like a pagoda caught mid-twist. Step inside and the strangeness becomes structural: the interior is a true double helix, two separate spiraling ramps that wind around each other so that visitors going up and visitors coming down never cross paths, never meet, never share a single step. Conceived by a temple priest named Ikudo, the hall was once lined with statues of the thirty-three Kannon of the Saikoku pilgrimage, so that one slow circuit through its coiling passage counted as completing a journey that would otherwise span half of western Japan — a pilgrimage in miniature, compressed into a single screw of wood. There are other "turban-shell halls" scattered across eastern Japan, but none pulls off the trick as audaciously as this one: a Leonardo-esque double-spiral staircase, built entirely in timber, more than three centuries ago, by craftsmen who left no blueprint behind. It was designated a National Important Cultural Property in 1995, and it remains one of the most genuinely uncanny pieces of architecture in the country.
highlights_en: A true wooden double-helix ramp where ascending and descending visitors never cross paths
Hexagonal three-story form, 16.5 meters tall, with a distinctive twisted-pagoda silhouette
A single circuit of the interior once stood in for the entire thirty-three-temple Saikoku Kannon pilgrimage

name: 大谷資料館
name_kana: おおやしりょうかん
address: 栃木県宇都宮市大谷町909
fee: 大人800円／小・中学生400円／未就学児無料
summary_en: Descend thirty meters beneath the town of Oya in Utsunomiya and the world opens into something between a cathedral and a science-fiction set: a man-made cavern of roughly 20,000 square meters—big enough to swallow a baseball stadium—carved out over some seventy years of quarrying Oya stone, a soft pumice tuff, between 1919 and 1986. Vertical walls fall away into darkness, and the square pillars left at regular intervals to hold up the ceiling march off into the gloom like the columns of a buried temple. The air hangs at a steady 8°C year-round, and dramatic lighting turns the raw industrial space into a stage. Oya stone earned its fame as a facing material for Frank Lloyd Wright's old Imperial Hotel; here, the place it came from has been rediscovered as an underground theater, hosting concerts, art shows, weddings, and countless film and commercial shoots. It is a rare case where an industrial scar has become one of Japan's most quietly awe-inspiring interior spaces.
highlights_en: A vast underground space of about 20,000 square meters—large enough to hold a baseball stadium
A 'buried-temple' landscape of vertically hewn walls and regularly spaced square pillars
A man-made cavern that stays a cool ~8°C all year round
A theatrical underground hall used for concerts, art exhibitions, film/commercial shoots, and weddings

name: ロックハート城
name_kana: ろっくはーとじょう
address: 群馬県吾妻郡高山村中山5583-1
fee: 大人1,300円／中・高校生1,100円／小人（4歳〜小学生）600円
summary_en: Deep in the mountains of Gunma stands a genuine Scottish castle—not a replica, but the real Lockheart Castle, completed in 1829, dismantled stone by stone and rebuilt 9,000 kilometers from home. The improbable story began in 1987, when the actor Masahiko Tsugawa bought the decaying castle; in 1988 it was taken apart over seven months into some 4,000 numbered stones weighing 600 tons, then shipped to Japan via the Trans-Siberian Railway. A Numata stone company, Sampo, acquired it in 1992 and spent roughly 1.5 billion yen and 15,000 worker-shifts reassembling it, opening the site in 1993. Today the castle anchors a roughly 100,000-square-meter 'Marble Village,' where a stone church and other buildings compose an artificial medieval-European townscape. The spectacle is gloriously disorienting: an authentic old-world castle, exquisitely detailed yet wrenched from its context, presiding over a Japanese mountain village. It is a monument to bubble-era ambition—and a perennial favorite as a film and drama location.
highlights_en: A genuine Scottish castle, dismantled, relocated, and rebuilt—standing in a Japanese mountain village
The relocation saga: some 4,000 numbered stones transported and reassembled
A medieval-European-style townscape, including a stone church, spread across roughly 100,000 square meters
A surreal, otherworldly space used as a location for countless dramas and films

name: サンメッセ日南
name_kana: さんめっせにちなん
address: 〒887-0101 宮崎県日南市大字宮浦2650
fee: 大人（高校生以上）800円／中学生500円／4歳以上350円
hours: 9:30〜17:00（最終入園16:30）水曜定休（祝日・年末年始・GW・お盆等は営業）
summary: 日南海岸の丘陵に広がるテーマパークで、太平洋を背に並ぶ7体のモアイ像「アフ・アキビ」で全国的に知られる。これらはイースター島（ラパ・ヌイ）の長老会から世界で初めて正式な複製許可を得て造られた完全復刻像であり、本物そっくりの石像が南国の海を見下ろす光景は、本来の文脈から切り離された不思議な景観を生んでいる。園内には地球感謝の鐘、彩色された人型オブジェ「ヴォワイアン」、展望広場、世界昆虫館などが点在し、「太陽からのメッセージを受けて地球に許される生き方に気づく」という独自のコンセプトを掲げる。観光地化されたモアイ像と思想性の強い施設運営が同居する、宮崎を代表するB級景観スポットである。
highlights: 太平洋を背に一列に並ぶ7体の完全復刻モアイ像（アフ・アキビ）
イースター島長老会から世界初の正式複製許可を得て造られたという来歴
海・空・芝生を背景にした南国×イースター島という文脈のずれた絶景
彩色された人型オブジェ「ヴォワイアン」など独自の造形物が点在する
from_kameyama: 車＋フェリーで約7〜8時間（名阪国道・新名神→大阪南港よりフェリーで宮崎港→国道220号で日南）。現実的には新幹線で新大阪→鹿児島中央、または宮崎空港まで空路の上、宮崎市から車で約30分。
summary_en: Perched on a hill above Japan's Nichinan coast, Sun Messe Nichinan is best known for a row of seven Moai statues facing out over the Pacific. These are no ordinary roadside imitations: they are full-scale replicas of Easter Island's famous "Ahu Akivi" Moai, built with what the park describes as the first formal permission ever granted by the council of elders of Rapa Nui (Easter Island) to reproduce the statues. Authorized copies of the Moai are vanishingly rare anywhere in the world, which makes a legally sanctioned set sitting on a Japanese clifftop genuinely strange — sacred ancestor-figures of a distant Pacific island, lifted out of their original meaning and re-staged against subtropical sea and sky. The park doubles down on the oddness with an earnest, almost spiritual mission statement ("to realize a way of living the Earth permits, by receiving the message of the sun") and fills its grounds with a Bell of Gratitude to the Earth, painted humanoid figures called "Voyant," observation decks, and an insect museum. The result is one of Miyazaki's signature scenic curiosities: a place where a faithful piece of borrowed sacred art, mass tourism, and homegrown New Age philosophy all share the same grassy hilltop.
highlights_en: Seven full-scale replica Moai (Ahu Akivi) standing in a row against the open Pacific
Reportedly the world's first officially permitted Moai reproductions, sanctioned by the Rapa Nui council of elders
A genuinely dislocating sight — Easter Island ancestor-figures re-staged in subtropical Japan with sea, sky, and lawn
Eccentric extras scattered across the park, including painted humanoid 'Voyant' figures and a Bell of Gratitude to the Earth

name: 大石神ピラミッド
name_kana: おおいしがみぴらみっど
address: 〒039-1801 青森県三戸郡新郷村大字戸来字雨池11
fee: 無料
hours: 見学自由（冬季は積雪により通行不可）
summary: 青森県新郷村戸来の山間にある巨石群で、周囲約12mの巨石を中心に「方位石」「太陽石」「星座石」「鏡石」などと名づけられた石が点在する。昭和9〜10年（1934〜35年）、画家の鳥谷幡山が「故郷にもピラミッドがある」として、日本各地の山をピラミッドと認定していた酒井勝軍を招き、調査の結果これを「日本ピラミッド」のひとつと認定したことで知られる。竹内文書に基づき、エジプトより古い数万年前の太陽礼拝所だとする主張がなされ、同村にある「キリストの墓」とともに新郷村のミステリースポットの双璧をなす。考古学的裏付けはなく、地質学的には自然の岩塊と解されるが、巨石信仰と昭和の超古代史ブームが交差した文化現象として注目される。
highlights: 周囲約12mの巨石を中心に複数の巨石が点在する山中の遺構
「方位石」「太陽石」「星座石」「鏡石」と名づけられた個々の巨石
酒井勝軍が認定した「日本ピラミッド」のひとつという来歴
巨石群の入口に立つ朱塗りの鳥居と説明板
from_kameyama: 現実的には新幹線（名古屋→東京→八戸、約5〜6時間）の後、八戸駅から車で約45分。車のみなら東北自動車道経由で約12時間。
summary_en: Deep in the mountains of Shingo Village in Aomori sits a cluster of large boulders that one strand of early-20th-century Japan decided was a pyramid older than the Egyptian ones. The centerpiece is a megalith roughly 12 meters around, surrounded by stones earnestly named the 'direction stone,' 'sun stone,' 'constellation stone,' and 'mirror stone.' The story dates to 1934, when an Aomori-born painter, Toriya Hatayama, insisted his homeland had a pyramid too and brought in Sakai Katsutoki — a man busy designating mountains all over Japan as ancient pyramids — who duly certified Mt. Oishigami as Japan's second 'pyramid.' The whole thing is woven from the Takenouchi Documents, a body of pseudo-ancient texts claiming Japan holds seven primordial sun-worship pyramids predating Egypt; the boulders, it's said, point exactly to the compass directions and bear carved ancient script and star maps. There is, of course, no archaeological or geological basis — these are natural rocks — but that misses the point. Together with the village's other famous oddity, the 'Tomb of Christ' just down the road, Oishigami is a perfectly preserved fossil of prewar occult nationalism fused with rural tourism: ancient sacred-rock instinct meets 1930s pyramid mania, marked today by a tidy vermilion torii gate and an explanatory signboard.
highlights_en: A mountain site centered on a megalith about 12 m in circumference with other boulders scattered around
Individually named megaliths — 'direction stone,' 'sun stone,' 'constellation stone,' 'mirror stone'
Certified by Sakai Katsutoki as one of Japan's 'pyramids' in 1934
A vermilion torii gate and explanatory board standing at the entrance to the megaliths

name: 葦嶽山
name_kana: あしたけやま
address: 広島県庄原市本村町
fee: 無料（登山）
hours: 見学自由（日中の登山推奨。冬季積雪期は注意）
summary: 広島県庄原市東部にそびえる標高815mの山で、古来「神武天皇陵」と伝えられてきた。どの方向から見ても三角形に見える山容から、昭和9年（1934年）にピラミッド研究家・酒井勝軍が「世界最古・2万3千年前のピラミッド本殿」と認定し、全国の新聞で報じられて「日本ピラミッド」の第1号として広く知られるようになった。向かい合う鬼叫山（ききょうさん）を拝殿とし、山頂付近の磐境（いわさか）や、鬼叫山に点在する鏡岩・方位石・ドルメン（供物台）・神武岩などの巨岩群が「神殿の痕跡」とされる。考古学的裏付けはなく自然の山と解されるが、神武天皇陵伝承と昭和の超古代史ブームが重なった「日本ピラミッド」発祥の地として独特の存在感をもつ。
highlights: どこから見ても三角形に見える端正なピラミッド型の山容（標高815m）
酒井勝軍が認定した「日本ピラミッド第1号」という発祥地の来歴
鬼叫山に点在する鏡岩・方位石・ドルメン・神武岩などの巨岩群
山頂付近の磐境（太陽石を囲む環状の石組み）
from_kameyama: 車で約4時間（名阪国道・新名神・中国道経由で庄原ICへ、さらに登山口の駐車場へ。登山は往復約3時間）。
summary_en: Mt. Ashitake, an 815-meter peak in the hills of eastern Shobara, Hiroshima, is the place where Japan's entire 'pyramid mountain' mythology began. Locals had long held it to be the tomb of Emperor Jimmu, Japan's legendary first emperor, but in 1934 the occultist and self-styled pyramid researcher Sakai Katsutoki arrived, looked at a mountain that reads as a clean triangle from every angle, and pronounced it 'the world's oldest pyramid — the main sanctuary, 23,000 years old.' The facing peak, Mt. Kikyo, he declared the worship hall. His theory was elegant in its way: real pyramids, he argued, simply used existing mountains, and only in flat, mountainless places like Egypt did people have to stack stones to fake one. Newspapers nationwide ran with it, and Mt. Ashitake became Japanese Pyramid No. 1, spawning a string of imitators — Mt. Minakami, Mt. Kuromata, the Oishigami stones. On neighboring Mt. Kikyo you'll find the supposed evidence: a 'mirror rock,' a 'direction stone,' a dolmen-like offering table, the 'Jimmu rock,' and a stone ring near the summit said to be a sun altar. None of it survives archaeological scrutiny — these are natural rocks and a natural hill — but as the birthplace of the Japanese pyramid craze, where imperial legend, ancient rock-worship, and 1930s pseudo-history all converge on one triangular peak, it remains genuinely singular. Reaching the megaliths takes a roughly three-hour round-trip hike.
highlights_en: A trim, pyramid-shaped peak (815 m) that reads as a triangle from every direction
The original 'Japanese Pyramid No. 1,' certified by occultist Sakai Katsutoki in 1934
Megaliths scattered on neighboring Mt. Kikyo — mirror rock, direction stone, dolmen, Jimmu rock
A stone ring near the summit said to be a sun altar (iwasaka)

name: お花大権現（林下寺）
name_kana: おはなだいごんげん（りんげじ）
address: 〒779-4702 徳島県三好郡東みよし町中庄2271
fee: 境内・拝観自由（祈祷・御朱印は別途）
hours: 境内拝観自由（堂内拝観は要確認）
