summary: 徳島県東みよし町にある真言宗御室派の寺院・林下寺の通称で、「お花さん」「お花大権現」として親しまれる性信仰の霊場。本尊は文殊菩薩、宝光山文殊院と号し、阿波西国観音霊場第10番札所でもある。江戸後期、岡山・津山藩の家老に仕え正妻の嫉妬により非業の死を遂げた女性「お花」が、「腰から下の病を治す性の御仏になる」と告げて祀られたと伝わる。堂内には大小無数の男根（男性のシンボル）の奉納物が山積みになり、その中央にお花さんの白い立像が立つ独特の景観で知られる。縁結び・夫婦和合・子授・安産・婦人病・性病平癒のご利益で「西日本一の性の神様」とされ、性器信仰・性病治癒祈願という日本の民俗信仰を今に伝える貴重なスポットである。
highlights: 堂内に山積みになった大小無数の男根（男性シンボル）の奉納物
奉納物に囲まれて立つお花さんの白い立像
「腰から下の病を治す性の御仏」という由来をもつ性信仰の霊場
縁結び・夫婦和合・子授・安産・性病平癒の「西日本一の性の神様」
from_kameyama: 車で約3時間30分（名阪国道・新名神・神戸淡路鳴門道・徳島道経由で井川池田ICへ）。または鉄道でJR三加茂駅下車、徒歩・タクシー。
summary_en: Officially Rinkaji is a respectable Shingon Buddhist temple in rural Tokushima, enshrining Manjusri, the bodhisattva of wisdom, and serving as the 10th stop on a regional Kannon pilgrimage. But almost nobody calls it that. It is known instead as 'Ohana-san' or Ohana Daigongen — and its main hall is piled high with countless carved wooden phalluses, a mountain of votive offerings surrounding the white standing figure of Ohana herself. The legend is part ghost story, part fertility cult: in the late Edo period, Ohana was a beautiful, gifted woman serving a chief retainer of Tsuyama domain in Okayama. The lord's favor toward her enraged his principal wife, who had Ohana murdered. Ohana's spirit then returned with a bargain — enshrine me as a deity, and I will heal 'the illnesses below the waist' for all people. So she became a god of sex, and grateful believers have answered ever since with phallic offerings, building the hall's astonishing accumulation. The result is one of western Japan's foremost sex-worship shrines, prayed to for matchmaking, marital harmony, conception, safe birth, and relief from venereal and women's diseases. It is also a quietly fascinating overlap of two Japanese folk traditions: phallic fertility worship layered onto goryo belief, in which a wronged woman who died violently is enshrined and appeased as a powerful god.
highlights_en: A hall piled high with countless carved phallic votive offerings of every size
The white standing figure of Ohana-san at the center of the offerings
A sex-worship site whose origin lies in a deity who 'heals the diseases below the waist'
Western Japan's foremost 'sex deity,' prayed to for matchmaking, conception, safe birth, and cure of disease

name: 秋田の聖母マリア像（聖体奉仕会）
name_kana: あきたのせいぼまりあぞう（せいたいほうしかい）
address: 〒010-0822 秋田県秋田市添川湯沢台1番地
status: 現存（カトリック在俗修道会・聖体奉仕会 聖堂内に安置）
fee: 拝観無料（聖堂は信仰の場。志納・献金は任意）
summary: 秋田市郊外の山あい、添川湯沢台に建つカトリック在俗修道会「聖体奉仕会」の聖堂に安置された木彫の聖母マリア像。1975年1月4日から1981年9月15日にかけて計101回にわたって「涙」を流したと記録され、拭き取った脱脂綿は秋田大学・岐阜大学の法医学教室で「ヒト体液」と鑑定されたとされる。1984年に新潟教区長が巡礼を禁じない旨の声明を出し、1988年にバチカン教理省が受理した。世界的に知られる「涙を流すマリア」の現場である。
highlights: 1975〜1981年に計101回の流涙が記録された木彫マリア像が、現在も聖堂に安置されている
拭った涙が法医学鑑定で「ヒト体液」と判定されたという特異な経緯
右回廊に聖ヨセフ像、左回廊に聖母像を配した、宮大工が建てた純日本建築の聖堂
ローマ・カトリック教会が巡礼を禁じない立場をとった、世界的にも稀な近現代の聖母現象の現場
from_kameyama: 鉄道＋新幹線で約7〜8時間（亀山→名古屋→東京→秋田、秋田駅からタクシー/車で約25分）。
name_en: The Weeping Madonna of Akita (Seitai Hoshikai Convent)
summary_en: On a quiet wooded hillside on the outskirts of Akita stands the chapel of Seitai Hoshikai — the Handmaids of the Eucharist, a Catholic secular institute — and within it, a single wooden statue of the Virgin Mary that the world came to know for crying. Beginning on the morning of January 4, 1975, the statue is said to have wept human tears 101 times over six and a half years, ending on September 15, 1981, the Memorial of Our Lady of Sorrows. Each time, the sisters gathered before the image to pray the rosary, and a priest wiped the tears away; the cotton used was reportedly examined at the forensic laboratories of Akita University and Gifu University and identified as human bodily fluid. The statue itself was carved in 1963 by a local Akita sculptor, Saburo Wakasa, and the austere, wholly Japanese-style chapel that houses it was built over seven years by a master shrine carpenter from Gunma — Mary in the left cloister, Saint Joseph in the right. In 1984 the Bishop of Niigata declared that the supernatural character of the events could not be denied and permitted pilgrimage; in 1988 the statement was received by the Vatican's doctrinal office under the future Pope Benedict XVI. It remains one of the very few modern Marian phenomena to draw a measured nod from Rome — and pilgrims still come, from Japan and far beyond, to sit in silence before a statue that is said to have grieved.
highlights_en: The wooden Madonna recorded as weeping 101 times between 1975 and 1981 is still enshrined in the chapel
The unusual record that wiped tears were forensically identified as human bodily fluid
A wholly Japanese-style chapel built by a shrine carpenter, with Mary in the left cloister and Saint Joseph in the right
One of the rare modern Marian phenomena toward which the Roman Catholic Church took a non-prohibitive stance on pilgrimage

name: 男鹿のゴジラ岩
name_kana: おがのごじらいわ
address: 〒010-0535 秋田県男鹿市船川港本山門前（潮瀬崎）
status: 現存（自然の海食岩。男鹿半島・大潟ジオパーク 潮瀬崎ジオサイト）
fee: 無料
hours: 24時間（磯場のため日中・干潮時推奨）
summary: 男鹿半島南西端、門前地区の潮瀬崎の磯場に立つ奇岩。約3,000万年前の火山礫凝灰岩が波と風雨に削られ、怪獣ゴジラが頭をもたげたようなシルエットを残した。男鹿半島・大潟ジオパークの潮瀬崎ジオサイトを構成し、周囲にはゴジラの尻尾岩・ガメラ岩・双子岩などの奇岩が点在する。夕暮れ時に口元へ夕陽が重なると火を噴くゴジラのように見え、じゃらん『行ってみたい夕日絶景ランキング』（2018年）で全国2位に選ばれた。
highlights: 頭をもたげた怪獣そっくりの自然岩のシルエット（火山礫凝灰岩の風化造形）
夕陽が口元に重なる「火を噴くゴジラ」の瞬間（4月頃・10月頃がベスト）
ゴジラの尻尾岩・ガメラ岩・双子岩など、周囲に点在する奇岩群（日本の奇岩百景）
波食台が隆起した磯場というジオパークとしての地形的見どころ
from_kameyama: 車で約8〜9時間。新幹線利用なら亀山→名古屋→東京→秋田、秋田駅から車で約60分。
summary_en: On the wave-battered rocky shore at the southwestern tip of the Oga Peninsula, a single chunk of 30-million-year-old volcanic tuff has been carved by wind and sea into the unmistakable profile of a monster rearing its head — Godzilla, mid-roar. The likeness is uncanny enough that the rock has become Oga's signature sight, the anchor of the Shiosezaki Geosite within the Oga Peninsula–Ogata Geopark, surrounded by a small bestiary of other named formations: Godzilla's Tail, Gamera Rock, the Twin Rocks, Hokakeshima. The real show comes at dusk. For a few weeks around April and October, the setting sun drops to exactly the height of Godzilla's open mouth, and for a moment the silhouette appears to breathe fire over the Sea of Japan — an image so reliably spectacular that photographers line the shore for it, and that earned the spot 2nd place nationwide in a 2018 ranking of Japan's most coveted sunset views. There is no entry gate, no ticket, no opening hours: just a slippery tidal platform, a monster made of stone, and the timing of the tide and the sun.
highlights_en: A natural rock whose silhouette uncannily resembles a monster rearing its head (weathered volcanic lapilli tuff)
The 'fire-breathing Godzilla' moment when the setting sun aligns with its mouth (best around April and October)
A cluster of strange rocks nearby — Godzilla's Tail, Gamera Rock, the Twin Rocks (100 Strange Rocks of Japan)
A geopark landform: an uplifted wave-cut platform shore

name: 唐人駄場遺跡（唐人石巨石群）
name_kana: とうじんだばいせき（とうじんいしきょせきぐん）
address: 〒787-0316 高知県土佐清水市松尾977
status: 現存（遺跡。唐人駄場園地として整備）
fee: 無料
hours: 見学自由（夜間・悪天候時は危険のため日中推奨）
summary: 足摺半島の先端近く、海岸段丘上に広がる縄文〜弥生期の遺跡。縄文早期（紀元前5000年頃）から弥生にかけての石器・土器片が出土する。隣接する尾根には高さ6〜7mの花崗岩巨石が林立する『唐人石』があり、円弧状に並ぶ列石はストーンサークル状とも指摘される（長径約185m・短径約160m）。真南を指すとされる『亀石』など人為を思わせる巨石も含み、太陽祭祀や縄文灯台の観点から市教委が調査した。考古学とロマンが交差する四国最南端のミステリースポット。
highlights: 高さ6〜7mの花崗岩巨石が林立する『唐人石』の圧倒的なスケール
円弧状に並ぶ列石（ストーンサークル状とされる配列）
真南を指すという『亀石』、尖った『鬼の包丁』など見立てられた巨石群
縄文早期〜弥生の遺物が出土する遺跡という考古学的背景と、太平洋を見下ろす眺望
from_kameyama: 車で約6時間。鉄道なら高知駅から特急＋バスで半日がかり。
summary_en: Near the southern tip of Shikoku, on a high coastal terrace above the Pacific, the Tojindaba site is two things at once — and refuses to settle into either. It is a genuine archaeological site, yielding stone tools and pottery shards spanning from the Early Jomon period (around 5000 BCE) into the Yayoi era. And it is a field of granite giants: on the ridge beside it stand the megaliths of Tojin-ishi, some 6 to 7 meters tall, with stones arranged in a sweeping arc that has been described as a stone circle measuring roughly 185 by 160 meters — which, if ever confirmed as deliberate, would be remarkable in scale. Among them are named stones heavy with implied intent: a "Turtle Stone" said to point due south, a jagged "Demon's Kitchen Knife." In 1993 the local board of education studied the rocks for signs of sun worship and even a possible Jomon-era beacon. Geologists will tell you this is simply what granite does when joints and weathering have eons to work; the megalith enthusiasts will tell you something older and stranger. Standing among the stones with the ocean below, you are free to believe whichever you like — which is exactly the appeal of the southernmost mystery in Shikoku.
highlights_en: The overwhelming scale of the Tojin-ishi megaliths — granite boulders up to 6–7 m tall
An arc of standing stones described as a stone-circle-like alignment
Named megaliths such as the south-pointing 'Turtle Stone' and the pointed 'Demon's Kitchen Knife'
An archaeological backdrop of Early-Jomon-to-Yayoi finds, with sweeping Pacific views

name: 大魚神社の海中鳥居
name_kana: おおうおじんじゃのかいちゅうとりい
address: 佐賀県藤津郡太良町多良1874-9（沖ノ島地先）
fee: 無料
hours: 見学自由（干潮時のみ徒歩接近可・潮汐表確認必須）
summary: 佐賀県藤津郡太良町、有明海に面した沖ノ島の地先に立つ三基の朱塗りの鳥居。最大約6mという日本最大級の干満差をもつ有明海の浅瀬に建てられているため、満潮時には海中に没して上部だけが水面に浮かび、干潮時には足元の干潟が露わになって鳥居の下をくぐって歩いて近づける。時刻と潮位によって景観が大きく変化する点が最大の特徴で、太良町は「月の引力が見える町」を掲げる。約300年前、悪代官を救った大魚（ナミウオ）への感謝に由来する大魚神社の祭祀施設であり、海の守り神として、三十年ごとに一基ずつ建て替える習わしが今も受け継がれている。
highlights: 三基の朱鳥居が有明海の浅瀬に一直線に並び、潮の満ち引きで海中に沈んだり干潟に立ち上がったりする
最大約6mという日本最大級の干満差により、同じ場所が時刻ごとにまったく別の景観に変貌する
干潮時には鳥居の足元まで歩いて近づき、鳥居をくぐることもできる
三十年ごとに一基ずつ建て替える伝統が現在も継承されている
from_kameyama: 車＋鉄道で約7〜8時間（亀山→新幹線で博多、博多からJR長崎本線・特急で肥前鹿島、太良町中心部へバス／タクシー）。自家用車なら名神・山陽・九州道経由で約8時間。
summary_en: On the tidal flats of the Ariake Sea in Tara Town, Saga Prefecture, three vermilion torii gates stand in a line out from the offshore islet of Okinoshima. The Ariake Sea has one of the largest tidal ranges in Japan — up to roughly six meters — so at high tide the gates sink into the sea until only their upper beams float on the surface, while at low tide the bared mudflats let you walk right out and pass beneath them. The defining feature is this radical transformation of the same scene from hour to hour; Tara Town brands itself as "the town where you can see the pull of the moon." The gates belong to Oouo ("Great Fish") Shrine, whose origin legend tells of a tyrannical magistrate saved from a drowning islet by a great fish; in gratitude he founded the shrine and erected torii in the sea. Honored as a guardian of the sea, the shrine still observes a custom of rebuilding one torii every thirty years.
highlights_en: Three vermilion torii aligned across the Ariake shallows, submerging into the sea or rising from the mudflats with the tide
A tidal range of up to about six meters — among Japan's largest — turns the same place into an entirely different scene hour by hour
At low tide you can walk right up to the gates and pass beneath them
A living tradition of rebuilding one torii every thirty years

name: 斎場御嶽
name_kana: せーふぁうたき
address: 沖縄県南城市知念久手堅539
fee: 大人（高校生以上）300円、小人（小・中学生）150円、6歳以下無料、団体（20名以上）200円
summary: 沖縄本島南部・南城市にある琉球王国最高位の聖地（御嶽）。2000年に「琉球王国のグスク及び関連遺産群」の構成資産として世界文化遺産に登録された。御嶽とは琉球の自然信仰における祈りの場で、社殿や偶像はなく、自然の岩・森・湧水そのものを神域とする。斎場御嶽の象徴が「三庫理（サングーイ）」で、二枚の巨岩が寄りかかって三角形のトンネル状の空間をつくり、その奥に久高島を遥拝する拝所がひらける。最高神女・聞得大君の就任儀礼などが営まれた女性祭祀の中枢であり、いまも信仰の生きる場として、写真撮影や立ち入りに地元のマナーが求められる。
highlights: 二枚の巨岩が三角形に寄りかかった「三庫理」のトンネル状空間が最大の見どころ
社殿も偶像もなく、岩・森・湧水そのものを神域とする琉球独特の御嶽信仰
三庫理の奥から神の島とされる久高島を遥拝できる
最高神女・聞得大君の就任儀礼が営まれた女性祭祀の中枢で、世界文化遺産
from_kameyama: 鉄道＋航空で半日強（亀山→名古屋→那覇空港、那覇から車・バスで南城市まで約50分）。沖縄本島のため陸路接続は不可。
summary_en: In Nanjo City, southern Okinawa, lies the supreme sacred site (utaki) of the former Ryukyu Kingdom, inscribed in 2000 as a component of the World Cultural Heritage "Gusuku Sites and Related Properties of the Kingdom of Ryukyu." An utaki is a place of prayer in Ryukyuan nature worship: it has no shrine building or idol, treating natural rock, forest, and springwater themselves as the sacred domain. Sefa-utaki's emblem is the Sangui, where two great slabs of rock lean together to form a triangular tunnel-like space, beyond which opens a place to worship the sacred island of Kudaka from afar. It was the heart of a female priesthood — the site of the inauguration rite of the highest priestess, the Kikoe-okimi — and as a still-living place of faith it demands local etiquette of visitors in their photography and movement.
highlights_en: The tunnel-like triangular space of the Sangui, where two great rocks lean together, is the foremost highlight
A distinctively Ryukyuan form of utaki worship with no shrine or idol — the rock, forest, and springs themselves are the sacred domain
From beyond the Sangui you can worship Kudaka Island, regarded as the island of the gods, from afar
A World Heritage site that was the heart of a female priesthood, where the highest priestess (Kikoe-okimi) was inaugurated

name: ガンガラーの谷
name_kana: がんがらーのたに
address: 沖縄県南城市玉城前川202
fee: ガイドツアー 大人2,500円／学生1,500円（学生証提示）／小学生以下無料（保護者同伴）。ガイド料・ドリンク代込
summary: 沖縄本島南部・南城市にある、数十万年前に鍾乳洞が崩落してできた亜熱帯の谷。約14,500坪の谷全体が自然と遺跡のフィールドで、予約制ガイドツアーでのみ立ち入れる。最大の見どころは「大主（ウフシュ）ガジュマル」で、崖の上から無数の気根を滝のように垂らしながら高さ約20mに育った巨大なガジュマルが、森の主のような威容を見せる。谷の奥の「武芸洞」では約2万3千年前の旧石器時代の人骨や貝製の釣り針が発見され、沖縄に渡来した「港川人」とのつながりも注目される、考古学的にも重要な場所。入口の鍾乳洞をそのまま使った「ケイブカフェ」も名物。
highlights: 崖上から無数の気根を垂らし高さ約20mに育った巨大ガジュマル「大主（ウフシュ）ガジュマル」
鍾乳洞が崩落してできた、亜熱帯の森と岩肌が一体化した谷の地形
旧石器時代の人骨や世界最古級の貝製釣り針が出土した武芸洞（港川人ゆかり）
入口の鍾乳洞をそのまま客席にした「ケイブカフェ」
from_kameyama: 鉄道＋航空で半日強（亀山→名古屋→那覇空港、那覇空港から車で約30分）。沖縄本島のため陸路接続は不可。
summary_en: In Nanjo City, southern Okinawa, a subtropical valley formed by the collapse of a limestone cave hundreds of thousands of years ago. The whole valley — about 14,500 tsubo — is a field of nature and ancient sites, accessible only by reserved guided tour. The greatest highlight is the Ufushu Gajumaru, a giant banyan that has grown to about 20 m while trailing countless aerial roots like a waterfall from atop the cliff, towering like a lord of the forest. In the Bukei Cave deep in the valley, Paleolithic human remains and a shell fishhook dated to about 23,000 years ago were discovered, drawing attention for the connection to the "Minatogawa Man" who reached Okinawa — making this an archaeologically important place. The "Cave Cafe," set in the entrance cave itself, is another signature feature.
highlights_en: The giant Ufushu Gajumaru banyan, grown to about 20 m while trailing countless aerial roots from atop the cliff
Valley terrain where subtropical forest and rock faces merge, formed by a collapsed limestone cave
The Bukei Cave, which yielded Paleolithic human remains and a shell fishhook among the world's oldest (linked to Minatogawa Man)
The "Cave Cafe," using the entrance limestone cave itself as seating

name: 筑波山ガマ石
name_kana: つくばさんがまいし
address: 茨城県つくば市筑波（女体山頂付近）
fee: 無料（筑波山ケーブルカー・ロープウェイは別途有料）
hours: 見学自由（屋外・常時／登山道上）
summary_en: On the ridge between the twin peaks of Mount Tsukuba sits a boulder that looks uncannily like a giant toad mid-croak, jaws thrown wide open. This is Gama-ishi, Toad Rock, and the resemblance is pure geology: the rock is gabbro, fractured in many directions as its magma cooled deep underground, then sculpted into a gaping maw by long ages of weathering once it reached the surface. Legend, of course, added a story. In the mid-Edo period a poor medicine peddler named Nagai Heisuke is said to have meditated before this rock for seven days and there devised the famous 'toad-oil seller's patter,' a piece of theatrical street salesmanship that became a regional tradition, giving the rock its name (it was originally the more sober 'Male Dragon Stone'). Today hikers passing on the summit trail keep the toad fed in a different way, tossing pebbles toward the hollow of its mouth in the belief that a stone that lands and stays will grant a wish, leaving a small cairn of hopes inside the stone toad's jaws. It is one of the celebrated strange rocks of the Mount Tsukuba Area Geopark.
highlights_en: A boulder whose silhouette mimics a wide-mouthed toad mid-croak
A folk belief that a pebble landed in its 'mouth' grants a wish, fed by an endless stream of thrown stones
The reputed birthplace of the 'toad-oil seller's patter,' a piece of Edo street-performance history

name: 楯築遺跡
name_kana: たてつきいせき
address: 岡山県倉敷市矢部
fee: 無料
summary_en: On a residential hill in Kurashiki stands something that looks less like an archaeological site than a Bronze Age stone circle: five great megaliths ringing the summit of an earthen mound, raised some 1,800 years ago. This is the Tatetsuki Site, a mounded tomb of the late Yayoi period (second half of the 2nd century CE) and one of the largest in all Japan, with a strange 'double square-circle' plan some 83 meters long. Excavations in the 1970s and 80s revealed, beneath the megaliths, a wooden coffin lined thickly with red cinnabar, an iron sword, and thousands of glass beads, the burial of a powerful chieftain from the age of Queen Himiko. Even more remarkable is the arc-pattern stone unearthed here, its entire surface carved with whirling band designs that prefigure the ritual patterns of the later kofun tombs; it survives today as the sacred body of Tatetsuki Shrine on the summit. Designated a National Historic Site in 1981, Tatetsuki is the rare place where you can stand among genuine megaliths, free and unfenced, on the threshold between Yayoi society and the coming age of the great tombs.
highlights_en: Five megaliths standing in a ring on the summit, an uncanny stone-circle-like landscape
An unparalleled 'double square-circle' form roughly 83 m long, among the largest of all Yayoi mounded tombs
An arc-pattern ritual stone covered in whirling motifs (national Important Cultural Property), enshrined as the sacred body of Tatetsuki Shrine

name: 玉比咩神社（立石）
name_kana: たまひめじんじゃ（たていし）
address: 〒706-0001 岡山県玉野市玉5-1-17 玉比咩神社境内
fee: 無料
hours: 境内自由（社務所対応は日中）
summary_en: Tamahime Shrine in Tamano, Okayama, enshrines Toyotama-hime — daughter of the sea-god and the "Otohime" princess of the undersea Dragon Palace — but its true centerpiece is the Tateishi, a single granite boulder roughly 10 meters tall and 30 meters around that rises directly in front of the main hall, girded with a sacred shimenawa rope. This is iwakura worship in its purest form: the rock itself is the deity, no carving, no statue, just the raw stone honored as a vessel of the divine. The shrine's name surfaces in Heian-era records, and the boulder — once a coastal landmark known as the "jewel stone" — is said to have given the city of Tamano its name. Local legend holds that three fireballs once burst from the stone, flying to Garyusan, the Saidaiji Kannon, and Ushimado, leaving round hollows that remain to this day. Because it honors the sea-princess who gave birth in the ocean palace, the shrine is also a place of prayer for safe childbirth and marriage. Standing at the foot of a rock the height of a three-story building, in a quiet seaside town, visitors consistently describe the same reaction: sheer awe.
highlights_en: A granite boulder roughly 10 meters tall and 30 meters around rising in front of the hall — the sacred body itself, viewable up close
The shimenawa-girt stone embodies the archetype of iwakura (megalithic rock-altar) worship, honoring the rock itself as a deity
Hollows atop the stone said to mark where three fireballs burst forth, flying to three nearby sacred sites (Garyusan, Saidaiji, Ushimado)
Safe-childbirth and matchmaking faith tied to the enshrined sea-princess Otohime (Toyotama-hime)

name: 源宗坊寺
name_kana: げんそうぼうじ
fee: 無料（参拝自由）
hours: 境内自由（日中の参拝が望ましい）
summary_en: Tucked into a hillside above the naval port of Kure, Gensoboji is a temple founded in 1906 by a wandering ascetic named Inada Gensobo — and almost everything you see here, he built himself. After years of austerities (he is said to have lived on tree roots and buds in a cave on the grounds), Gensobo vowed to heal the sick, and then, entirely self-taught, began bending rebar and troweling on mortar to make Buddhist statues. Roughly 35 of them now stand scattered across the slope — Yakushi the medicine Buddha, a four-armed Fudo Myoo, an eleven-headed Kannon, dragon kings, wind gods — about 13 of them his own work, painted in startling, saturated color. The centerpiece is a six-meter Fudo Myoo gazing toward Kure Bay, itself an "inner Buddha" that Gensobo meant to encase inside an even larger Shakyamuni. He never finished it: today only the giant's left hand and left foot remain on the hillside, an eerie monument to an unrealized vision. Crude and powerful in equal measure, the statues have been reappraised in recent years as a striking example of outsider art and handmade folk devotion.
highlights_en: Roughly 35 vividly painted concrete statues, self-taught and hand-built by the founder, scattered across a mountain slope
A six-meter Fudo Myoo facing Kure Bay — itself an inner Buddha nested within a never-completed giant
The surreal remnants of the unfinished giant Shakyamuni: only its left hand and left foot survive
Faces unlike any professional sculptor's — crude, forceful, and intensely personal (true outsider-art devotion)

name: 「ん！」看板（国道42号沿い）
name_kana: んかんばん
address: 三重県度会郡大紀町（国道42号沿い、正確な地区は非公表）
fee: 無料（公道沿いの広告看板・外観のみ）
summary: 三重県度会郡大紀町の国道42号沿いに立つ、ひらがな2文字「ん！」とだけ大書された巨大な縦長看板。設置したのは多気郡明和町に本社を置く建築会社・株式会社小川で、約10年前に立てられたとされる。
highlights: ひらがな「ん！」の2文字だけを巨大に掲げた、意味を拒否する縦長看板
山肌の法面（のり枠・落石防護柵）を背景に、青空へ突き立つ白い看板の異様な存在感
下部の「建築のことなら何でもどうぞ！」「広告主募集中」とのギャップが生む脱力感
from_kameyama: 車で約1時間20分（名阪国道→伊勢自動車道→紀勢自動車道大宮大台IC、または国道42号を南下）。鉄道はJR紀勢本線で大紀町方面へ。
summary_en: Somewhere along National Route 42 in Taiki Town stands a tall white billboard that says, in two enormous black hiragana characters, only "ん！"—roughly "n!", the kind of grunt of surprise that isn't even a word. There is no place name, no slogan up top, nothing to decode; just that single syllable and an exclamation mark, looming against a netted mountain slope and the open sky. The fine print at the bottom finally explains it, sort of: it is an ad for Ogawa Co., Ltd., a construction company based in nearby Meiwa Town, complete with "anything about construction, leave it to us!", a phone number, and—best of all—a line reading "advertisers wanted." Asked why, the company president told J-Town Net in 2023, "There's no meaning to it (laughs). It's an eye-catching design—isn't that fun?" Erected about a decade ago, the sign has since drifted around social media as Taiki Town's "mystery billboard," a perfect specimen of roadside-advertising minimalism: a monument whose entire content is the bare act of making you go "huh?"
highlights_en: A giant vertical billboard bearing only the two hiragana characters 'ん！'—a sign that refuses to mean anything
The eerie presence of a white sign jutting into the blue sky against a netted, frame-reinforced mountain slope
The deflating gap between the cryptic 'N!' and the mundane 'anything about construction' / 'advertisers wanted' fine print below

name: 大丹倉
name_kana: おおにぐら
address: 三重県熊野市育生町大森（大森神社からカシノト道経由・大丹倉山頂へ）
status: 現存（自然の名勝・行場）
fee: 無料
hours: 見学自由（日中の明るい時間帯推奨）
summary_en: Daitankura is a colossal rock wall—roughly 300 meters high and 500 meters wide—rising deep in the mountains of Kumano, Mie Prefecture. The "tan" in its name means red, and true to that, the rock face glows reddish: the iron within this rhyolite mass has oxidized through weathering, staining the cliff a deep rust color that stands out starkly against the surrounding green. Since ancient times it has been a sacred ground for Shugendō ascetics, and it preserves a tradition of giant-rock (iwakura) worship that took root here even before pilgrims began flocking to the Kumano Sanzan; at its foot, Tankura Shrine still enshrines a great boulder as its sacred body. From the end of the forest road the summit is only about a five-minute walk, and a trail also runs via Omote-tankura—but the summit is a fenceless natural precipice, and footing is entirely at the visitor's own risk. It is a living site where nature worship and mountain austerity converge in one vivid red cliff.
highlights_en: A roughly 300 m × 500 m rock wall stained red by oxidized iron, towering above the forest at overwhelming scale
A working ground of primeval mountain faith—iwakura rock worship and Shugendō austerity predating the Kumano Sanzan pilgrimage
At its foot, Tankura Shrine enshrines the boulder itself as a deity, a nature-worship lineage still alive today

name: ジャンボ干支（辰水神社）
name_kana: じゃんぼえと（たつみずじんじゃ）
address: 三重県津市美里町家所1941
status: 現存（毎冬・期間限定で巨大干支を展示）
fee: 無料
hours: ジャンボ干支の展示は毎年おおむね12月29日〜2月中旬（年により2月末〜3月末まで延長）。展示期間外は通常の山中の神社として参拝自由
summary_en: Every winter since 1986, the small Tatsumizu Shrine in the hills of Misato, Tsu City, sprouts a giant—the "Jumbo Zodiac." A local volunteer group, the Furusato Aikōkai, builds that year's zodiac animal at far-larger-than-life-size from a steel frame sheathed in carved, painted Styrofoam, and dedicates it on the shrine's approach as a "good-fortune passage gate" you walk beneath to pray for the new year. Each figure stands roughly three meters tall and wide, and—crucially—it is rebuilt from scratch every year, so the creature changes completely: a dragon that puffs white gas from its mouth one year, a 3.2-meter white serpent clutching a gold coin the next, a rearing white-and-gold horse for 2026. On display only from around December 29 to mid-February, this handmade colossus appearing in a snowbound mountain shrine is a beloved winter tradition of central Mie—and, with its aging makers, one whose survival is now uncertain.
highlights_en: A three-meter-class zodiac figure of steel and Styrofoam appears on the approach as a walk-through "good-fortune gate"
Rebuilt for that year's animal every year, so the design and staging (gas-spewing mouths, coin-clutching beasts) transform annually
A winter-only tradition handmade and dedicated by local volunteers (Furusato Aikōkai) since 1986

name: 河鹿荘（榊原温泉廃旅館）
name_kana: かじかそう（さかきばらおんせんはいりょかん）
address: 三重県津市榊原町（紫峰閣に隣接）
status: 現存（廃旅館。外観のみ視認可・敷地内立入不可）
fee: —（外観のみ・公道から）
hours: —（建物は閉鎖。見学する場合は昼間に公道から外観のみ）
summary_en: Kajikasō is the ruin of a hot-spring inn in Sakakibara Onsen, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture—a five-story concrete main building joined by a covered corridor to a two-story wooden annex. Records note it even ran a 1987 television commercial billed as "The Dalliance of the Celestials." Sakakibara is no ordinary spa: it has served as a site of ritual purification before pilgrimages to Ise for some 1,500 years and is identified with "Nanakuri no Yu," the spring named in Sei Shōnagon's "Pillow Book." Yet amid the post-bubble shift in tourism, Kajikasō and the adjacent former national lodge Shihōkaku—both closed—were left behind, leaving a riverside dotted with abandoned inns. It stands directly beside this site's existing entry, Shihōkaku. The building is heavily deteriorated and the grounds are off-limits; viewing should be confined to the exterior, from the public road, in daylight—no trespassing.
highlights_en: A large inn structure survives: a five-story concrete main block linked by corridor to a two-story wooden annex
One of a cluster of abandoned inns along the river, telling the rise and decline of the ancient Sakakibara hot spring
Located right next to this site's existing entry, Shihōkaku—a distinctive spa-town landscape of consecutive ruined inns

name: 日原鍾乳洞
name_kana: にっぱらしょうにゅうどう
address: 〒198-0211 東京都西多摩郡奥多摩町日原1052
fee: 大人（高校生以上）900円、中学生700円、小学生600円（25人以上で1人100円割引）
summary: 東京都西多摩郡奥多摩町、秩父多摩甲斐国立公園の山中にある関東地方最大級の鍾乳洞。総延長は約1,270メートル、高低差は約134メートルに及び、観光コースだけでも数百メートルを巡る規模を誇る。江戸時代以前から知られ、かつては山岳信仰の聖地として修験者や行者が籠もったと伝わり、洞内には白い観音像が安置されるなど宗教的痕跡を今に残す。長い年月をかけて形成された鍾乳石や石筍が連なる地下空間に、近年はカラフルな照明や水琴窟の演出も加わり、都心から日帰り圏とは思えない別世界の景観をつくり出す。真夏でも洞内は概ね11度前後と冷涼で、避暑地としても知られる。
highlights: 総延長約1,270m・高低差約134mに及ぶ関東地方最大級の地下空間
数百年をかけて成長した鍾乳石・石筍が連なる巨大ホールと奇岩
山岳信仰の名残として洞内に安置された白い観音像
色とりどりの照明と水琴窟が演出する非日常の地下景観
真夏でも約11度前後と冷涼で避暑地としても知られる環境
from_kameyama: 鉄道なら亀山駅からJR関西本線・東海道新幹線・中央線等を乗り継ぎ青梅線「奥多摩駅」へ約5〜6時間、さらに西東京バス「東日原」下車徒歩約30分。車なら東名阪・新東名・圏央道経由で青梅方面へ約6時間、奥多摩の狭隘な山道に注意。
summary_en: Deep in the mountains of Okutama, within Chichibu-Tama-Kai National Park on Tokyo's far western edge, Nippara Limestone Cave is one of the largest caverns in the entire Kanto region. With a total length of about 1,270 meters and a vertical drop of around 134 meters, the visitor route alone winds for hundreds of meters through a vast underground world. Known since before the Edo period, the cave was once a sacred site of mountain worship where ascetics and pilgrims secluded themselves, and it still bears traces of that devotion — a white Kannon statue stands enshrined within. Stalactites and stalagmites built up over centuries crowd the great halls, and in recent years colorful lighting and a suikinkutsu water chime have been added, conjuring a landscape that feels impossibly remote for somewhere within a day trip of central Tokyo. Even at the height of summer the interior hovers around 11 degrees Celsius, making it a celebrated retreat from the heat.
highlights_en: One of Kanto's largest underground spaces, about 1,270 m long with a 134 m vertical difference
Great halls lined with stalactites and stalagmites grown over hundreds of years, and strange rock formations
A white Kannon statue enshrined inside as a vestige of mountain worship
An otherworldly subterranean landscape staged with colored lighting and a suikinkutsu water chime
An environment that stays around 11°C even in midsummer, known as a cool-off retreat

name: 大船観音寺
name_kana: おおふなかんのんじ
fee: 大人（高校生以上）300円、小・中学生100円、団体（20名以上）200円、未就学児無料
summary: 神奈川県鎌倉市岡本、大船駅西口の高台にそびえる曹洞宗の寺院で、全高約25メートルの巨大な白衣観音像で知られる。特徴的なのは、この観音像が全身像ではなく上半身（胸像）であること。丘の上から穏やかに街を見下ろす真っ白な観音の顔と肩だけが、駅のホームや市街地のいたるところから忽然と姿を現し、その独特のシルエットは大船のシンボルとなっている。昭和初期に建立が始まり、戦争で中断したのち昭和35年（1960年）に完成した近代の大造仏で、像内（胎内）に入って拝観することもできる。曹洞宗大本山總持寺の直末で、本尊は聖観世音菩薩。平和を祈る巨像として親しまれている。
highlights: 全高約25mの巨大な白衣観音像が「胸像（上半身のみ）」という珍しい造形
丘の上から街を見下ろす真っ白な顔と肩だけが各所から忽然と現れるシルエット
像内（胎内）に入って拝観できる近代の大造仏
昭和初期着工・戦争中断を経て1960年完成という建立の歴史
大船駅ホームや市街地から見上げられる大船のランドマーク
from_kameyama: 鉄道なら亀山駅からJR・東海道新幹線で「新横浜」または「小田原」経由、JR東海道線・横須賀線「大船駅」へ約2時間半〜3時間、西口から徒歩約10分（坂・階段あり）。車なら新名神・新東名・圏央道・横浜横須賀道路経由で約4時間半。
summary_en: Rising from a hilltop just west of Ofuna Station in Kamakura, Kanagawa, this Soto Zen temple is famous for its colossal white-robed Kannon statue standing about 25 meters tall. What makes it remarkable is that the figure is not a full body but only the upper half — a bust. From the hill, only the gleaming white face and shoulders of the bodhisattva gaze gently down over the town, appearing without warning from train platforms and street corners all across the city, a singular silhouette that has become the symbol of Ofuna. Construction began in the early Showa era, was interrupted by the war, and was finally completed in 1960 — a modern colossal Buddhist image that visitors can even enter and view from within. A direct branch of the great Soto Zen temple Sojiji, its principal object of worship is the Sho-Kannon Bodhisattva, beloved as a giant statue praying for peace.
highlights_en: A colossal white-robed Kannon about 25 m tall, rendered in the unusual form of a bust (upper body only)
A silhouette in which only the gleaming white face and shoulders appear from points all over town, gazing down from the hill
A modern colossal Buddhist image that visitors can enter and view from within
A construction history of early-Showa groundbreaking, wartime interruption, and completion in 1960
A landmark of Ofuna, looked up to from the station platforms and across the town

name: 牛久阿弥陀大佛（牛久大仏）
name_kana: うしくあみだだいぶつ
address: 茨城県牛久市久野町2083
summary_en: Ushiku Daibutsu in Ushiku City, Ibaraki, is a colossal standing statue of Amida Buddha 120 meters tall (a 100 m figure on a 20 m pedestal). Built by Higashi Honganji of the Jodo Shinshu sect in a region tied to its founder Shinran, it was begun in 1986 and completed in 1993. Clad in over 6,000 bronze plates and weighing about 4,000 tons, it is recognized by Guinness World Records as the world's tallest bronze Buddha. Its defining feature is overwhelming scale-the Great Buddha of Nara would fit in its palm. The interior is a five-story pilgrimage facility, with an elevator rising to a chest-level observation deck at 85 meters; the 480 curls on its head are each 1 m wide and weigh some 200 kg. Surrounded by an expansive garden with a petting zoo and seasonal flower fields, it stands as an icon of modern Japan's monumental-Buddha landscape.
highlights: 全高120m、ブロンズ立像として世界最大（ギネス世界記録認定）
奈良の大仏が掌に乗るほどの圧倒的スケールの阿弥陀如来立像
青銅板6000枚以上・総重量約4000トン・螺髪480個（1個約200kg）
地上85mの胸部展望台までエレベーターで上れる5階構造の胎内拝観
ふれあい動物公園や季節の花畑を擁する広大な庭園との対比
highlights_en: 120 m tall, the world's largest bronze standing statue (Guinness World Record)
An Amida Buddha of overwhelming scale-the Nara Great Buddha would fit in its palm
Over 6,000 bronze plates, about 4,000 tons, and 480 head curls (about 200 kg each)
A five-story interior pilgrimage with an elevator to an 85 m chest-level observation deck
A striking contrast with the vast garden's petting zoo and seasonal flower fields

name: 立岩（しあわせ祈岩）
name_kana: たていわ（しあわせきがん）
address: 山口県大島郡周防大島町鹿家（立岩ヶ浜）
fee: 無料
hours: 見学自由（海岸沿い・常時開放）
summary: 瀬戸内海・周防大島（屋代島）の海岸に、高さ約40mの安山岩が一本の塔のようにそそり立つ奇岩。古くから男性を象徴する「男岩（夫岩）」と崇められ、岩の下部には農耕の守り神・馬頭観音がまつられる。雄立岩・雌立岩と呼ばれる一対の岩塔のうち背の高い方で、約1300万年前の瀬戸内火山活動で生まれた硬い高マグネシア安山岩が周囲の風化に耐えて残った自然の造形物。周防大島には立岩・巌門・帯石・岩屋の四つの奇岩があり、これらを巡って夫婦和合・家庭円満・子孫繁栄・健康長寿を願う「仕合わせ祈願（しあわせ祈岩めぐり）」の信仰が伝わる。性象徴と石への信仰、観音信仰が重なった、瀬戸内の素朴な民俗世界の核心を体感できる場所。
highlights: 海岸から垂直に立ち上がる高さ約40mの巨大な一枚岩・男岩（夫岩）
雄立岩・雌立岩の一対構成で、男女・夫婦和合を象徴する性信仰の岩
岩の下部に農耕の守り神・馬頭観音がまつられる石＋観音の重層信仰
立岩・巌門・帯石・岩屋を巡る『仕合わせ祈願』四奇岩めぐりの一番札
from_kameyama: 車で約4時間（亀山IC→新名神・山陽道で玖珂IC、国道437号で大島大橋を渡り周防大島へ／約330km）。鉄道はJR関西本線・東海道新幹線等で広島方面、JR山陽本線「大畠駅」下車後はバス・タクシーで島内へ（公共交通は便数が少なく車が現実的）。
name_en: Tateiwa Rock (Shiawase Kigan, the Lucky Prayer Rock)
summary_en: On the coast of Suo-Oshima (Yashiro Island) in the Seto Inland Sea, a roughly 40-meter-tall andesite rock soars upward like a single tower. Long venerated as the "male rock" (otto-iwa, the husband rock) symbolizing the masculine, it has a Bato Kannon — the Horse-Headed Kannon, guardian deity of agriculture — enshrined at its base. It is the taller of a pair of pinnacles known as the male Tateiwa and the female Tateiwa, a natural sculpture of hard high-magnesia andesite born of Setouchi volcanic activity about 13 million years ago that survived while the surrounding rock weathered away. Suo-Oshima has four curious rocks — Tateiwa, Ganmon, Obiishi, and Iwaya — and a folk belief called "Shiawase Kigan" (the pilgrimage of the lucky prayer rocks) survives, in which people circuit them to pray for marital harmony, household peace, prosperity of descendants, and good health and longevity. It is a place where one can feel the heart of the Seto Inland Sea's unpretentious folk world, in which sexual symbolism, stone worship, and Kannon devotion overlap.
highlights_en: A massive single rock, the male rock (husband rock), rising vertically about 40 m from the coast
Paired as male Tateiwa and female Tateiwa, a rock of fertility belief symbolizing the harmony of the sexes and of married couples
Layered devotion of stone and Kannon, with the agricultural guardian Bato Kannon enshrined at the rock's base
The first stop of the 'Shiawase Kigan' four-curious-rock pilgrimage of Tateiwa, Ganmon, Obiishi, and Iwaya

name: 神戸岩
name_kana: かのといわ
address: 東京都西多摩郡檜原村神戸8020
fee: 無料
summary: 神戸岩（かのといわ）は、東京都檜原村の北秋川支流・赤井沢にある小渓谷で、両岸に高さ約100mの岩盤が屏風のように切り立つ奇勝である。約2億5千万年前のジュラ紀の硬質なチャート（火打ち石）層からなり、川水や風雨の浸食に耐えて、わずか数メートルの狭い谷と垂直の岸壁を残した。扉のように開きかけて見える岩の延長線上に大嶽神社があることから、ここを神域への入口とみなし「神の戸（かみのと）」と呼んだのが名の由来とされる。東京都にありながら秘境然とした景観で、1960年に東京都の天然記念物に指定された。岩壁沿いには鎖場やはしご、木橋が設けられ、スリリングな渓谷散策ができるパワースポットとして知られる。
highlights: 両岸に約100mの高さでそそり立つチャートの垂直岸壁と、扉状に切れ込んだ狭隘な谷
約2億5千万年前のジュラ紀チャート層が浸食に耐えて残した地質学的奇景
鎖場・はしご・木橋を伝って岩壁の間を進むスリリングな渓谷散策路
from_kameyama: 車で約6時間（新東名・首都圏中央連絡道経由、あきる野ICから檜原村方面）。日帰りは現実的でなく東京観光と組み合わせる前提。
summary_en: Two hours west of central Tokyo, where the suburbs surrender to mountains, a stream called Akaisawa has carved a slot so narrow you can almost touch both walls at once — and those walls rise about 100 meters straight up. This is Kanotoiwa, a gorge cut into Jurassic chert laid down some 250 million years ago from the silica skeletons of deep-sea plankton. Chert is brutally hard, and where softer rock all around it was ground away by water and weather, this slab held its ground, leaving a vertical-walled corridor of stone that feels less like geology than architecture. Local belief reads the cleft as a doorway: a shrine, Ootake Jinja, stands along the line where the rock seems to be swinging open, so the place was named the "door of the god" — the threshold to a sacred realm. Designated a Tokyo Natural Monument in 1960, Kanotoiwa is improbably wild for a site inside the metropolis: chains, ladders and a wooden bridge let you thread the gorge itself, scrambling between cliffs in a slot of shadow, water and 250-million-year-old stone.
highlights_en: Vertical chert walls rising roughly 100 meters on both sides, framing a slot-narrow gorge that looks like a door cut into the mountain
A geological curiosity where 250-million-year-old Jurassic chert resisted erosion to leave a sheer-walled corridor
A thrilling gorge walk threading between the cliffs by chains, ladders and a wooden bridge

name: 鵜戸神宮
name_kana: うどじんぐう
address: 〒887-0101 宮崎県日南市大字宮浦3232
fee: 無料（参拝自由）
from_kameyama: 車＋フェリー等で片道おおむね半日以上（亀山から大阪南港・神戸等経由で宮崎港へ、宮崎ICから国道220号で約50分）。航空機利用なら宮崎空港から路線バスで約60分が現実的。
summary_en: On the cliffs of the Nichinan coast in Miyazaki, where the Hyuga Sea hammers the rock, the shrine of Udo Jingu does something almost no other shrine in Japan does: it hides. To reach the vermillion main hall you do not climb toward the gods — you descend, down a stone stairway cut into the cliff face, into the mouth of a vast sea cave (38 meters east-to-west, 8.5 meters high) where the lacquered building sits tucked beneath a ceiling of raw rock. This is a 'kudari-miya,' a descending shrine, and the effect is uncanny: the architecture of worship swallowed into the body of the earth. Behind the hall, two breast-shaped protrusions called the 'milk rocks' (ochichi-iwa) drip 'milk water,' and legend holds the shrine's deity — said to be the father of Japan's first emperor — was nursed on candy made from that water. Out on the rocks, pilgrims hurl small clay 'fortune balls' (untama) at a hollow in the Turtle Stone: men with the left hand, women with the right. Cave, breast-rocks, and the strange throwing rite fuse into one of Kyushu's most visually arresting sites of folk belief.
highlights_en: A vermillion main hall set inside an enormous sea-eroded cave, 38 m wide and 8.5 m high
A rare 'descending shrine' (kudari-miya): you go down stone steps to worship rather than up
The breast-shaped 'milk rocks' and dripping 'milk water' tied to a legend of nursing the infant deity
The untama rite — flinging clay fortune balls into a hollow in the Turtle Stone for a wish

name: 秋芳洞
name_kana: あきよしどう
address: 〒754-0511 山口県美祢市秋芳町秋吉3449-1
fee: 大人（高校生以上）1,600円／中学生1,300円／小学生850円（2026年4月1日改定・団体割引あり）
from_kameyama: 亀山から名神・中国自動車道経由で約7〜8時間。小郡萩道路『秋吉台IC』から車で約5分。遠方のため一泊以上の旅程が現実的。
summary_en: Beneath the rolling karst grassland of Akiyoshidai — Japan's largest limestone plateau — lies a void that water spent eons hollowing out. Akiyoshido runs 10,300 meters in total, the third-longest cave in the country, and about a kilometer of it is opened to visitors as a path that plunges 100 to 200 meters below daylight. What you walk into does not feel like a single room; it feels like a geology that has gone strange. The signature sight is the 'Hundred Plates' (Hyakumai-zara), a vast cascade of rimstone pools terraced like flooded rice paddies, on a scale almost unmatched anywhere in the world. Above the path rises the 'Golden Pillar' (Ogon-bashira), a 15-meter flowstone column built grain by grain from dripping calcite, the cave's emblem. Add the 5-meter 'Cave Fuji' and a procession of forms too large to read as anything but otherworldly. The cave was once known by the plain name 'Takiana,' the waterfall hole, until the future Emperor Showa visited in 1926 and it was rechristened Akiyoshido. It is a National Special Natural Monument — and one of those rare places where a single photograph tells you exactly why it belongs among the strange.
highlights_en: A giant limestone cave over 10 km long, third-largest in Japan, designated a National Special Natural Monument
The world-renowned 'Hundred Plates' — terraced rimstone pools cascading like rice paddies
The 'Golden Pillar,' a roughly 15-meter flowstone column that is the cave's symbol
A procession of uncanny underground forms including the 5-meter 'Cave Fuji' pillar

name: 香山昇龍大観音
name_kana: こうやましょうりゅうだいかんのん
address: 〒838-1521 福岡県朝倉市杷木志波871-4
fee: 無料（拝観自由）
from_kameyama: 亀山から福岡県朝倉市まで車で片道おおむね9〜10時間（名神・山陽・九州道経由）。現地は西鉄バス『杷木』から車で約10分の山上。遠方のため一泊以上の旅程が現実的。
name_en: Koyama Shoryu Dai-Kannon (Koyama Ascending-Dragon Great Kannon)
summary_en: On a hilltop above Haki in Asakura, Fukuoka, a 28-meter goddess of mercy rises from the back of a coiling dragon — and the whole improbable thing exists because of a dream. Hayashi Hifumi ran the inn Taisenkaku at Harazuru hot spring and served four terms as mayor of Haki town, and he credited his fortune in business and politics to a Kannon who appeared to him in his sleep and told him what to do. In thanks, at the age of 75, he spent his own money to raise this colossus, completed in August 1987. It claims to be the largest 'ascending-dragon Kannon' in Japan: the bodhisattva standing serene atop a dragon spiraling toward heaven, verdigris-green against the sky. People come to pray for money and prosperity, and the statue is marketed locally as 'Asakura's power spot.' But strip away the brochure language and what you have is purer and stranger — a single man's gratitude and success, cast in concrete at monumental scale and parked on a mountain, the kind of place where one photograph tells the whole story.
highlights_en: A 28-meter giant Kannon billed as Japan's largest 'ascending-dragon Kannon'
The unusual form of the bodhisattva standing atop a heaven-bound coiling dragon
Built with the private funds of a hot-spring innkeeper who followed a Kannon's dream revelation
Venerated as a 'power spot' for blessings of wealth and prosperity

name: サウナ発達
name_kana: さうなはったつ
address: 〒975-0008 福島県南相馬市原町区本町3-21
fee: 日帰りプラン11,700円〜／1泊2食付23,400円〜（税・サ込・1日1組貸切。予約時に要確認）
summary: サウナ発達は、福島県南相馬市原町区の市街地に建つ、1日1組完全予約制の体験型サウナ施設。元お笑い芸人で実家の米穀店「川口商店」を継いだ川口雄大が、土嚢を積み上げて土壁を築く「アースバッグ工法」で自ら建てた洞窟状のサウナを核とする。サウナ室は土の蓄熱による高湿・岩盤浴的な熱環境を持ち、地元海岸に漂着した流木なども建材に用いられる。隣接する水風呂は、壁一面を世界各国の仮面で埋め尽くした異様な空間で、地下水を掛け流す。同一敷地には飲食の川口商店、宿泊棟「宿巣」、建物内を継ぎ足しで装飾していく“継ぎ足し美術館”が一体化し、2025年からは建物全体を「神社（神社発達）」として登録する構想が進む。施設名の「発達」は、発達障害と『地域とともに発達する』という二重の意味を込めたもの。
highlights: 土嚢を積んで築いたアースバッグ工法の洞窟サウナ（土壁・高湿・蓄熱で岩盤浴的な熱環境）
壁一面を世界各国の仮面で埋め尽くした異形の水風呂（地下水掛け流し）
1日1組完全予約制で施設をまるごと貸し切る、珍しい運営形態
飲食(川口商店)・宿泊(宿巣)・継ぎ足し美術館が一体化した、オーナー自作の複合施設
2025年から建物全体を『神社（神社発達）』として登録する進行中の構想
from_kameyama: 車で約8時間（約650km・新東名〜首都高〜常磐自動車道、南相馬IC経由）。公共交通なら新幹線で東京・仙台経由、JR常磐線「原ノ町駅」下車で約6〜7時間。
summary_en: In a quiet block of central Minamisoma — a city on Fukushima's Hamadori coast that the world came to know only through disaster — a former stand-up comedian has spent a decade building, by hand, one of the strangest bathhouses in Japan. Sauna Hattatsu is the work of Takehiro Kawaguchi, who took over his family's earthquake-shuttered rice shop in 2014 and, after his teenage son announced he wanted to build his own house, taught himself the earthbag construction method: stacking soil-filled sacks into walls. The result is a genuine cave sauna of packed earth and local driftwood — humid, radiant, storing heat like a kiln — that owes more to the steam baths of ancient cave-dwellers than to any Finnish blueprint. Step from the heat into the cold bath and you are watched: every wall is crammed with carved masks from around the world, a wooden congregation staring down at the 13°C groundwater. The whole compound is a single self-built organism — an eatery (Kawaguchi Shoten), a lodging wing (Suka), and a 'keep-adding museum' whose toilets are wallpapered with manga clippings that guests are invited to extend. It runs for exactly one group per day, by reservation only, so the entire bizarre world is yours alone. And from 2025, Kawaguchi intends to register the entire building as a Shinto shrine — 'Jinja Hattatsu' — turning a homemade sauna into a place of worship still very much under construction.
highlights_en: A genuine earthbag cave sauna built by hand from soil-filled sacks and local driftwood — humid, radiant, kiln-like heat unlike any dry sauna
A cold bath whose every wall is covered with carved masks from around the world, watching over 13°C flowing groundwater
Booked one group per day, by reservation only — you rent the entire bizarre compound to yourselves
A single owner-built organism fusing an eatery (Kawaguchi Shoten), a lodging wing (Suka), and an ever-growing 'keep-adding museum'
From 2025, an in-progress plan to register the whole building as a Shinto shrine ('Jinja Hattatsu')

name: 西福寺開山堂
name_kana: さいふくじかいさんどう
address: 〒946-0212 新潟県魚沼市大浦174
fee: 大人500円／中学生・障害者300円／小学生以下無料（20名以上1割引）
summary: 新潟県魚沼市大浦、曹洞宗・赤城山西福寺の境内に建つ開山堂。幕末から明治にかけて活躍し『日本のミケランジェロ』と称される石川雲蝶（いしかわうんちょう）が、堂内外を埋め尽くすように彫刻・絵画を残したことで知られる。最大の見どころは堂内の天井三間四方の全面を覆う大彫刻『道元禅師猛虎調伏の図』で、何層にも重なる透かし彫りと極彩色が織りなす立体的な迫力は、平面の絵画とも一般的な欄間彫刻とも一線を画す。日光東照宮に劣らぬとして『越後日光』とも呼ばれ、新潟県の文化財に指定されている。一人の職人が常軌を逸した密度と色彩で空間そのものを彫り尽くした、見る者を圧倒する“彫刻の宇宙”である。
highlights: 天井三間四方を覆う大彫刻『道元禅師猛虎調伏の図』の透かし彫り＋極彩色
『日本のミケランジェロ』石川雲蝶が堂内外を埋め尽くした圧倒的な密度
『越後日光』と称される茅葺の開山堂と彫刻群（新潟県文化財）
from_kameyama: 車で約5時間（東名阪〜名神〜北陸道〜関越道・小出IC）。日帰りは長距離
summary_en: In a snow-country temple in Uonuma, Niigata, one craftsman spent years carving an entire building into a single, overwhelming work of art. Saifukuji's Kaisando — the Founder's Hall of this Soto Zen temple — is the masterwork of Ishikawa Uncho, the late-Edo carver remembered as the 'Japanese Michelangelo.' Inside, the entire coffered ceiling, three bays square, erupts into a single colossal carving: 'Dogen Subduing the Fierce Tiger,' layer upon layer of openwork rendered in vivid, saturated color. It is neither a painting nor an ordinary transom carving but something between sculpture and fever dream — a three-dimensional picture scroll that swallows the room. Likened to the lavish gates of Nikko Toshogu, the hall is nicknamed 'Echigo Nikko' and protected as a Niigata cultural property. To stand beneath it is to be pinned in place by the sheer density of a single artisan's obsession, an entire cosmos chiseled overhead.
highlights_en: An entire ceiling, three bays square, carved into 'Dogen Subduing the Fierce Tiger' — layered openwork in saturated polychrome
The obsessive density of Ishikawa Uncho, the 'Japanese Michelangelo,' filling the hall inside and out
A thatched Founder's Hall nicknamed 'Echigo Nikko,' designated a Niigata cultural property

name: 中山仙境 無明橋
name_kana: なかやませんきょう むみょうばし
address: 大分県豊後高田市黒土（中山仙境登山道）
fee: 無料（登山）
hours: 常時開放（晴天の日中のみ推奨）
summary: 大分県豊後高田市、国東半島の奇岩が連なる岩稜帯『中山仙境（夷耶馬）』の稜線上に架かる石橋。標高こそ317mと低いが、六郷満山の山岳仏教・修験道の行場として知られ、屏風のように切り立つ凝灰岩の岩峰群が連なる。無明橋は二枚の自然石を拝み合わせに架けただけの簡素な石橋で、幅はわずか40〜50cm・欄干は一切なく、両側は断崖。そこへ至る稜線には『ナイフエッジ（馬の背）』と呼ばれる左右が切れ落ちた痩せ尾根もあり、豊後高田市自身が『滑落による死亡事故がたびたび発生』と注意を促す危険地帯となっている。信仰の行場が剥き出しの岩稜と一体化した、緊張感に満ちた“修行の橋”である。
highlights: 稜線上に二枚の自然石を架けただけの幅約40〜50cm・欄干なしの石橋『無明橋』
左右が切れ落ちる『ナイフエッジ（馬の背）』の痩せ尾根歩き
六郷満山・修験道の行場と一体化した屏風状の奇岩群（滑落死亡事故多発の危険地帯）
from_kameyama: 亀山市から車＋フェリー等で片道約8〜9時間（遠方）。九州・国東半島観光と組み合わせ前提
summary_en: On the Kunisaki Peninsula in Oita, a ridge of jagged tuff peaks rears up like a folding screen — Nakayama Senkyo, an old training ground of the Rokugo Manzan mountain-Buddhist and Shugendo tradition. It is only 317 meters high, yet it kills people. Strung across the very crest of the ridge is the Mumyo Bridge: two raw stones leaned together prayer-fashion, perhaps 40 to 50 centimeters wide, with no railing of any kind and a sheer cliff on either side. The approach crosses a knife-edge 'horse's back' where the ground falls away left and right. Bungotakada City itself warns, in writing, that 'falling deaths have occurred repeatedly here.' The name Mumyo — Buddhist 'ignorance,' the delusion one crosses beyond — is no accident: this is a bridge built for ascetics, where terror and faith are the same step. Tourism has put it on the map but changed nothing on the ground; there is still no pavement, and still no handrail.
highlights_en: The railing-less Mumyo Bridge — two natural stones, about 40–50cm wide — laid straight across the crest of the ridge
A knife-edge 'horse's back' ridge that drops away on both sides
Screen-like strange rock peaks fused with a Shugendo training ground — a danger zone with repeated falling deaths

name: 太郎坊宮（阿賀神社）
name_kana: たろうぼうぐう あがじんじゃ
address: 〒527-0091 滋賀県東近江市小脇町2247
fee: 拝観無料（駐車場無料／お守りづくり・祝詞書写体験は各2,000円）
summary: 滋賀県東近江市、独立峰・赤神山（あかがみやま、標高約350m）の中腹に鎮座する阿賀神社の通称。約1400年前、聖徳太子が霊山と感じて神を祀ったのが起源と伝わり、勝運・厄除けの神として「太郎坊さん」の名で親しまれる。最大の見どころは本殿直前にそびえる巨大な「夫婦岩（めおといわ）」で、高さ20mに迫る二つの大岩が左右から迫り、その間わずか約80cmの隙間が参道となっている。神力で押し開かれたと伝わるこの岩には「正直な者が通れば願いが叶い、悪心ある者は岩に挟まれる」という言い伝えがあり、嘘をつくと挟まれるという子どもへの戒めも残る。中世以来の修験道・天狗（太郎坊天狗）信仰の霊地でもあり、740段超の石段を登り切った断崖に社殿が建つ“天空の神社”として、巨岩と眺望が一体となった独特の聖地となっている。
highlights: 高さ20m級の巨岩が左右から迫る『夫婦岩』、その隙間わずか約80cmが参道
『悪心ある者は岩に挟まれる』という戒めの伝説が残る巨石信仰
740段超の石段を登った断崖に社殿が建つ“天空の神社”と太郎坊天狗の霊地
from_kameyama: 車で約1時間（新名神〜名神八日市IC、または名阪国道〜国道421号）
summary_en: On the flank of Mount Akagami, a steep isolated peak in eastern Shiga, stands Aga Shrine — known to everyone as Taroboo-gu, the shrine of the tengu. Founded, tradition says, some 1,400 years ago by Prince Shotoku, it is a god of victory and good fortune. But the reason people climb its 740-plus stone steps is the thing waiting just before the main hall: the Meoto-iwa, the Married Rocks. Two boulders nearly 20 meters high lean in from either side, and the only way forward is a slot barely 80 centimeters wide running straight between them. Legend says the gods pushed the rocks apart, and that an honest heart passes through to have its wish granted — while a wicked one is crushed where it stands. Generations of local parents have warned their children: lie, and the rocks of Taroboo-san will get you. With the cliffside hall perched at the top and the Higashiomi plain falling away below, it is a 'shrine in the sky' where megalith worship, mountain asceticism and tengu folklore fuse into one uncanny passage of stone.
highlights_en: The Married Rocks — two boulders nearly 20m high — close in from both sides, leaving a path barely 80cm wide squeezing between them
A megalith-worship legend that the honest pass through to have wishes granted, while the wicked are crushed by the rocks
A cliffside 'shrine in the sky' atop 740-plus steps, guarded in folklore by the Taroboo Tengu

name: 吉見百穴
name_kana: よしみひゃくあな
address: 〒355-0155 埼玉県比企郡吉見町大字北吉見324
fee: 中学生以上300円／小学生200円／未就学児無料（団体割引あり）
summary: 吉見百穴（よしみひゃくあな）は、埼玉県比企郡吉見町の凝灰質砂岩の丘陵斜面に掘られた古墳時代後期（6〜7世紀）の横穴墓群である。現在219基が確認され、斜面一面に方形の穴が蜂の巣状に開口する独特の景観をなす。1923年（大正12年）に国の史跡に指定された。明治期の発掘当初は先住民コロボックルの住居跡とする説もあったが、現在は横穴式の墓と理解されている。穴の一部にはヒカリゴケが自生し、1928年に国の天然記念物に指定された。さらに太平洋戦争末期には丘陵内部に大規模な地下軍需工場（トンネル）が掘られ、横穴墓の一部が破壊された。古代の墓・希少植物・戦争遺跡が一つの丘に重層する、考古・自然・近代史の交差点である。
highlights: 急斜面に蜂の巣状に開口する219基の横穴墓（古墳時代後期）
太平洋戦争末期に掘られた地下軍需工場の巨大トンネル
国の天然記念物ヒカリゴケが穴の奥で黄緑に光る
from_kameyama: 車で約5時間（新名神・伊勢湾岸・圏央道経由、約440km）
summary_en: On a sandstone hillside an hour north of Tokyo, a slope erupts into a honeycomb of square holes — 219 of them, cut into the rock more than 1,400 years ago. They are not dwellings, though Meiji-era scholars once insisted they were the homes of a legendary lost people; they are tombs, horizontal burial chambers from the late Kofun period, arranged across the cliff like the cells of an enormous hive. That alone would earn Yoshimi Hyakuana its strangeness. But walk to the right and the hill opens again — this time into a vast, blast-cut tunnel, part of an underground munitions factory dug in the desperate final months of the Pacific War, a project that destroyed a dozen of the ancient tombs to make room for aircraft parts. Add to this the luminous moss that glows faint yellow-green in the dark of one chamber, a nationally protected natural treasure, and you have a single small hill where Bronze-Age death, wartime total mobilization, and rare botany are stacked one atop another. Few places in Japan compress so much history, and so much eeriness, into one cliff face.
highlights_en: 219 ancient burial chambers honeycombing a single sandstone slope
A blast-cut WWII underground munitions tunnel carved into the same hill
Nationally protected luminous moss glowing faint green deep in one cave

name: 安神山の烏帽子岩
name_kana: あんじんさんのえぼしいわ
address: 愛媛県今治市大三島町宮浦（大山祇神社背後・安神山）
status: 現存（自由に登山可・神体山）
fee: 無料
hours: 見学自由（日中の登山推奨）
summary: 安神山（あんじんさん）は、瀬戸内しまなみ海道の大三島にそびえる標高約267〜275mの岩峰で、山頂部の花崗岩が烏帽子岩・兜岩・唐獅子岩などと呼ばれる巨岩・奇岩群をなす。とりわけ烏帽子岩は二枚の巨石が割れて立つ独特の姿で、その隙間から瀬戸内海の島々を望む。安神山は、全国の山祇神社・三島神社の総本社である大山祇神社（おおやまづみじんじゃ）の背後に立つ神体山（神が鎮まる山）の一つとされ、古来より信仰の対象とされてきた。花崗岩が露出した尾根には巨石が点在し、磐座（いわくら）信仰の気配を色濃く残す。登山口は大山祇神社で、安神山を経て大三島最高峰の鷲ヶ頭山（436.5m）へと尾根が続く。距離は短いが岩場が連続し、神域としての厳かさと瀬戸内の大展望を併せ持つ。
highlights: 二枚の巨石が割れて立つ烏帽子岩と、その隙間から望む瀬戸内海
兜岩・唐獅子岩など花崗岩の奇岩が点在する露岩の尾根
大山祇神社の神体山＝磐座信仰を色濃く残す神域
from_kameyama: 車で約3時間（新名神・山陽道・しまなみ海道経由で大三島へ、約230km）
summary_en: On Omishima, the largest island of Ehime and one of the stepping-stones of the Shimanami Kaido, a granite peak called Anjinsan rises behind the most important shrine in the region. Oyamazumi Shrine is the head of every Yamazumi and Mishima shrine in Japan, the war-god's shrine where samurai for a thousand years dedicated their armor, and the mountain at its back is not scenery but sanctuary — a shintaizan, a mountain that is itself the body of a god. Climb it and the divinity becomes literal and tactile. The summit ridge bristles with megaliths the locals have named for what they resemble: Kabuto-iwa, the helmet; Karajishi-iwa, the lion-dog; and above all Eboshi-iwa, two enormous granite slabs split clean down the middle and standing upright like a torn court cap, framing the blue islands of the Seto Inland Sea through the gap between them. This is iwakura worship in its rawest form — not a deity housed in a building but a boulder you stand before and revere. The hike from the shrine is short but the rock is real, a continuous scramble of warm pink granite, and at the top the sense that the stones themselves are watching is hard to shake.
highlights_en: Eboshi-iwa: two huge granite slabs split apart and standing upright, framing the Seto Inland Sea through the cleft
A ridge of exposed granite studded with named oddities — the helmet rock, the lion-dog rock
A living sacred body-mountain of Oyamazumi Shrine, steeped in primal iwakura (rock) worship

name: 釣石神社
name_kana: つりいしじんじゃ
address: 〒986-0201 宮城県石巻市北上町十三浜字菖蒲田305
fee: 無料（参拝自由）
hours: 境内自由（社務所は概ね日中）
from_kameyama: 車＋新幹線で片道およそ8〜9時間（名阪国道→東名・新東名で東京、東北新幹線で仙台、在来線＋三陸道で石巻・北上町）。日帰りは非現実的で、仙台・石巻泊が前提。
summary_en: On a wooded slope near the mouth of the Kitakami River in northeastern Miyagi, a single enormous boulder juts out of the cliff face and hangs there, girded by a thick sacred straw rope, looking for all the world like it is about to crash down onto the worshippers below. It never does. That is the entire premise of Tsuriishi Shrine — the name literally means "suspended stone." The upper boulder, roughly 14 meters around, is the male deity; a second stone below, about 8 by 4 meters, is the female deity; and the shrine itself is dedicated to Amenokoyane no Mikoto, the god of wisdom and learning. What turned a local rock into a nationally known pilgrimage site was catastrophe. In 1978 a magnitude-7.4 earthquake struck offshore and the boulder did not move. In 2011 the Great East Japan Earthquake sent a tsunami up the estuary that erased the surrounding village and swept away the shrine's office, its torii gate, and its ema hall — and still the stone did not fall. From that came its title: "the exam-passing god that looks about to fall but never does." The lower boulder is now armored in thousands of wooden votive tablets left by students, and a flight of 174 stone steps climbs past the rock to an inner sanctuary rebuilt in 1914. It is at once a place of ancient rock worship and a very modern engine of test anxiety.
highlights_en: A ~14-meter-circumference boulder juts from the cliff, roped like it is 'suspended,' looking perpetually about to fall — and never does
Having survived two great earthquakes and a tsunami, it became 'the exam-passing god'; the lower stone is buried under thousands of votive tablets
174 stone steps climb from directly beneath the boulder to a 1914 inner sanctuary, a living site of iwakura sacred-rock worship overlooking the Kitakami estuary

name: 立石
name_kana: たていし
address: 〒981-2321 宮城県伊具郡丸森町字泉（羽入地区）
fee: 無料
hours: 見学自由（山道・日中推奨）
summary: 宮城県丸森町羽入地区の山中に屹立する、高さ約12.5メートル・周囲約25メートルの巨大な花崗岩の一枚岩。丸森町指定の天然記念物で、地元では「日本一の道祖神」とも呼ばれる。木立の中を登山道でたどると、墓標のように空へ突き上がる黒い岩壁が突如として現れ、根元の人の背丈ほどの巨石を従えて立つ姿は圧巻である。周辺からは弥生土器の破片が出土しており、古代から自然崇拝の対象とされてきたと考えられている。安倍貞任がこの石の頂に立ったという伝承や、八幡太郎義家が立石から松掛の的石へ矢を射たという弓の逸話も残り、東北の巨石信仰と武将伝説が重なる稀有な磐座である。
highlights: 高さ約12.5m・周囲約25mの花崗岩の一枚岩が墓標のように屹立する異形
根元の巨石を従えて空へ突き上がる、写真一枚で伝わる圧倒的なスケール感
弥生土器が出土する古代自然崇拝の磐座であり『日本一の道祖神』とも呼ばれる
from_kameyama: 三重県亀山市から車で約9時間（新名神→伊勢湾岸→東名→常磐道・相馬ICまで約8時間＋一般道約1時間）。現実的には東北新幹線で白石蔵王駅・福島駅経由でレンタカー利用が便利。
summary_en: Deep in the wooded hills of the Hanyu district of Marumori, in southern Miyagi, a single slab of granite rises out of the forest like a black gravestone thrown up by the earth — roughly 12.5 meters tall and 25 meters around. Locals call it Tateishi, the "standing stone," and, with a straight face, "Japan's greatest dosojin" — the roadside guardian deity usually represented by a small stone figure at a village boundary, here inflated to the size of a five-story building. Follow the mountain trail up from the Hanyu community hall and the monolith appears suddenly through the trees, dwarfing a boulder the size of a person crouched at its foot. This is no mere geological curiosity. Pottery fragments scattered around its base — some said to be Yayoi-period — mark it as an iwakura, a sacred rock worshipped since prehistory. Layered on top are the war legends of ancient Tohoku: the rebel lord Abe no Sadato is said to have stood on its summit around 1050, and the legendary archer Minamoto no Yoshiie is said to have loosed an arrow from here across the valley to a distant "target stone." One rock, holding down both the animist deep past and the samurai epic — and doing it while looming over you like a wall.
highlights_en: A single granite monolith about 12.5m tall and 25m around, standing upright like a gravestone hurled from the earth
The overwhelming scale — captured in a single photo — as it towers over a person-sized boulder at its base
An ancient iwakura where Yayoi pottery has been found, known locally as 'Japan's greatest roadside guardian deity'

name: 越前がにミュージアム
name_kana: えちぜんがにみゅーじあむ
fee: 大人500円／小人（3歳以上〜小学生）300円（団体20名以上・障がい者割引あり）
hours: 9:00〜17:00（最終入館16:30）／休館：毎週火曜（夏休み期間は無休、11〜3月は第2・第4火曜）
summary: 越前海岸に建つ、カニ一点特化の体感型ミュージアム。2000年7月15日に開館した日本でも珍しいカニ専門博物館で、愛称は「ビックラブ」。何より目を引くのは建物そのもので、ドーム型の甲羅と左右に大きく広げたハサミを模した外観が、海に向かって身構える巨大な越前ガニのシルエットを描く。
highlights: 海に向かってハサミを広げた巨大な越前ガニを模した建物外観そのものが最大の見どころ
波しぶきまで再現した「かに漁チャレンジ漁船体験シミュレーター」で本物の漁船操舵を疑似体験
深海300mを三層吹き抜けで再現した巨大ジオラマ「パノラマ劇場」と幅10mスクリーンのビッグラブシアター
from_kameyama: 車で約2時間30分（名阪国道→北陸自動車道 鯖江IC→越前海岸方面）
summary_en: On the rugged Echizen coast of Fukui Prefecture stands a building shaped like a colossal crab bracing toward the sea — a domed 'shell' with two claw-like wings flung wide. This is the Echizen Gani Museum, one of Japan's very few museums devoted entirely to a single crustacean: the Echizen crab, the male snow crab that is Fukui's iconic winter delicacy and is still presented to the Imperial Household. Opened on July 15, 2000, and nicknamed 'BigLove' (a public-vote blend of bikkura — the shock of seeing a giant crab — plus Crab plus Love), the museum commits to its one subject with total conviction, from architecture to exhibits. Inside, a three-story atrium diorama recreates the sea floor at a depth of 300 meters; a 10-meter-wide, 3-meter-high screen in the 'BigLove Theater' projects crab-fishing footage; and a fishing-boat simulator — complete with reproduced sea spray — lets visitors pilot a crab boat through the entire voyage from harbor to catch and back. It is a place where earnest marine biology and unabashed B-kyu (roadside-attraction) spectacle share the same roof, and the giant-crab building alone makes it unmistakable from the road.
highlights_en: The building itself, shaped like a giant Echizen crab with claws spread toward the sea, is the single greatest sight
A fishing-boat simulator that reproduces even the sea spray lets visitors pilot a real crab-fishing voyage from departure to return
A three-story atrium diorama recreating the sea floor at 300 meters deep, plus the 10-meter-screen BigLove Theater

name: めがねミュージアム
name_kana: めがねみゅーじあむ
address: 〒916-0042 福井県鯖江市新横江2-3-4 めがね会館
fee: めがね博物館：入館無料（体験工房は有料・要予約）
hours: めがねSHOP 10:00〜19:00／体験工房・めがね博物館・カフェ 10:00〜17:00（水曜・年末年始休、祝日除く）
summary: 国産眼鏡フレームの約95%を生産する「めがねのまち」福井県鯖江市が、眼鏡ただ一点に特化して建てた専門ミュージアム。拠点となる「めがね会館」は屋上に赤い巨大めがねのオブジェを掲げ、真上から見ると建物そのものが眼鏡の形をしている。館内に入るとまず、約700個の金属フレームを組み上げた直径約3.2メートルの球体オブジェが頭上に浮かぶ。3階のめがね博物館は入館無料で、1905年に増永五左衛門が農閑期の副業として大阪から職人を招いたことに始まる100余年の生産史、福井発のチタンフレーム、めがね大使・大村崑が集めた美空ひばりや石原裕次郎ら著名人愛用眼鏡のコレクションを展示する。総合博物館ではなく、一産業に街ぐるみで殉じた偏執の視覚化として尖っている。
highlights: 屋上に赤い巨大めがねオブジェを掲げ、真上から見ると建物自体が眼鏡形をした「めがね会館」
約700個の眼鏡フレームで組んだ直径約3.2メートルの球体オブジェが入口の頭上に浮かぶ
喜劇俳優・大村崑が集めた美空ひばり・石原裕次郎ら著名人愛用眼鏡の「有名人眼鏡コレクション」
from_kameyama: 車で約2時間15分（名神・北陸自動車道経由で鯖江ICから約10分）、または鉄道でJR鯖江駅下車徒歩約10分
summary_en: In a snowbound corner of Fukui Prefecture, an entire town has devoted itself to a single object: the eyeglass. Sabae produces roughly 95% of all frames made in Japan, and the Megane Museum is the shrine that obsession built. Its home, the Megane Kaikan ("Eyeglass Hall"), wears a giant red pair of glasses on its roof as a landmark, and — in a detail that borders on the fanatical — the building itself is shaped like a pair of spectacles when seen from directly above. Step inside and the first thing to greet you hangs overhead: a sphere about 3.2 meters across, assembled from roughly 700 metal frames, so that hundreds of pairs of glasses coalesce into one glowing orb. The free third-floor museum traces a century-plus of local history that began in 1905, when the farmer Masunaga Gozaemon imported Osaka craftsmen to give his neighbors off-season work; it also shows the titanium frames that Sabae pioneered, and a "celebrity eyewear collection" gathered by comedian and eyeglass ambassador Kon Omura, including glasses once worn by singer Hibari Misora and film star Yujiro Ishihara. This is not a general museum but the visualization of an industrial monomania — a place that speaks of nothing, and everything, but glasses.
highlights_en: The Megane Kaikan wears a giant red pair of glasses on its roof and is itself shaped like spectacles when viewed from directly above
A 3.2-meter sphere built from roughly 700 eyeglass frames hangs overhead at the entrance — hundreds of pairs of glasses fused into one orb
A 'celebrity eyewear collection' assembled by comedian Kon Omura, including glasses worn by Hibari Misora and Yujiro Ishihara

name: 勝山城博物館
name_kana: かつやまじょうはくぶつかん
address: 〒911-0806 福井県勝山市平泉寺町平泉寺85-26-1
fee: 大人700円（団体560円）／小中高校生280円（団体230円）
hours: 9:30〜16:30（入館は16:00まで）水曜休館・冬季休館あり
summary: 福井県勝山市の田園地帯に、天守風建築として日本一の高さ57.8メートルを誇る白亜の巨大城郭がそびえる。姫路城天守を模したコンクリート製の模擬天守で、内部は江戸時代の大名武具や合戦図屏風などを収める登録博物館。史実の勝山城には天守が造営されたことはなく、この城型建築は史跡の再現ではない。地元出身の実業家で相互タクシー創業者・多田清が私財を投じて建て、1992年に開館させた。石垣には九頭竜川と恐竜化石にちなむ9匹の龍が彫り込まれている。
highlights: 天守風建築として日本一の高さ57.8メートル・田園にそびえる白亜の巨大模擬天守
石垣に彫られた9匹の龍（九頭竜川と勝山産恐竜化石にちなむ）
史実の城とは無関係にタクシー会社創業者が私財で建てた5層6階の登録博物館
from_kameyama: 車で約2時間30分（名神・北陸自動車道→福井北IC経由・中部縦貫道勝山IC）
summary_en: Rising abruptly from the rice paddies of rural Fukui, this blinding-white castle keep is the tallest tenshu-style structure in Japan — 57.8 meters from stone base to golden ridge ornaments. It looks, from a distance, like a lost feudal fortress. It is not. No castle keep ever stood here in history; the medieval Katsuyama Castle had only a keep platform, never a tower, and its site has vanished. This gleaming five-tiered, six-story reinforced-concrete replica of Himeji Castle's keep was instead the private passion project of Tada Kiyoshi, a local boy who founded the Sōgo Taxi company and wanted to give his hometown 'a new face.' He poured his own fortune into it over three years and died in 1991, a year before it opened in 1992 as a registered museum housing daimyō armor, battle screens and Qing-dynasty embroidery. Look at the stone base and you'll find the giveaway that this is no ordinary castle: nine dragons carved in relief, honoring the Kuzuryū (Nine-Headed Dragon) River and the dinosaur fossils for which Katsuyama is famous. It is one of Japan's great mock keeps — a photograph of it raises the question 'why is there a giant castle here?' before you've read a single caption.
highlights_en: At 57.8 meters, the tallest tenshu-style structure in Japan — a colossal white mock keep looming over open farmland
Nine dragons carved into the stone base, honoring the Kuzuryū River and Katsuyama's dinosaur fossils
A five-tiered, six-story registered museum built with the private fortune of a taxi-company founder, on a site that never had a real castle keep

name: みなとつるが山車会館
name_kana: みなとつるがやまかいかん
address: 〒914-0062 福井県敦賀市相生町7番6号
fee: 一般300円／団体（20名以上）250円／高校生以下無料（博物館との共通券は一般500円）
hours: 9:00〜17:00（最終入館16:30）。月曜休（祝日の場合は開館し翌日休）・祝日の翌日・年末年始（12/29〜1/3）休
summary: 氣比神宮例大祭（敦賀まつり）で巡行する巨大な山車を、屋内でそのまま見上げられる展示施設。館内に収蔵された6基のうち3基を常設展示し、多層構造の山車の頂に、本物の甲冑・能面をまとった等身大の武者人形が戦国合戦の一場面を再現して載る。白馬に跨り弓を引き絞る武将像は天井近くまでそびえ、水引幕や松の依り代を含めた総高は建物2層分に達する。祭りでは大山車の依り代の松が高さ9m超に及んだと伝わる、その巨大さと戦国絵巻の生々しさを、雨や日程に左右されず間近で体感できる稀有な珍スポットである。上映最後に山車がせり出すスクリーンシアターも備える。
highlights: 屋内展示ホールに山車が2層分の高さでそびえ、頂の武者人形が天井近くまで届く圧倒的スケール
本物の甲冑・能面を着けた等身大の武者人形が、白馬に跨り弓を引く戦国合戦の一場面を再現
上映の最後に山車が観客席へせり出すスクリーンシアターと、鎧兜がずらり並ぶ甲冑展示室
from_kameyama: 車で約1時間20分（名神・北陸自動車道 敦賀ICから約10分）。公共交通なら亀山駅からJRで敦賀駅へ約2時間、敦賀駅から徒歩約25分またはコミュニティバス。
name_en: Minato Tsuruga Float Museum (Minato Tsuruga Yama Kaikan)
summary_en: Most festival floats you only glimpse once a year, rolling past in a crowd. Here, in a purpose-built hall in the old port town of Tsuruga, three of the six festival floats of the Kehi Shrine grand festival stand indoors year-round — and indoors, freed from the street, their sheer scale is startling. Each float is a multi-tiered wooden tower crowned by a life-sized warrior figure dressed in genuine armor and a real Noh mask, staging a frozen moment of a Warring States battle: one warrior rears back on a white horse, drawing a bow, the whole tableau climbing nearly to the ceiling, two building-stories tall above the visitors below. The armor is not costume but the real thing, as are the masks; a second-floor gallery lets you circle a suit of it at 360 degrees. In the old days the largest floats carried a sacred pine over nine meters high as a vessel for the descending god, so wide it filled the lane. The museum keeps that gigantism and the eerie theatrical realism of the warrior scenes on tap regardless of weather or date — a rare place to stand under a festival float and feel how monstrous it really is. A screen theater seals it, with a float sliding out toward the audience at the climax.
highlights_en: Floats standing two stories tall inside the exhibition hall, their warrior figures reaching nearly to the ceiling — a scale you never register from the street crowd
Life-sized warrior figures in genuine armor and real Noh masks, one rearing on a white horse mid-bowshot, staging a Warring States battle
A screen theater whose float slides out toward the audience at the climax, plus a gallery of real samurai armor lined up under blue light
